11.title: PRECAUTIONS
11.body: PRECAUTIONS GI-2 Observe the following precautions to ensure safe and proper servicing. These precautions are not described in each individual section. General Precautions Do not operate the engine for an extended period of time without proper exhaust ventilation. Keep the work area well ventilated and free of any inflammable materials. Special care should be taken when handling any inflammable or poisonous materials, such as gasoline, refrigerant gas, etc. When working in a pit or other enclosed area, be sure to properly ventilate the area before working with hazardous materials. Do not smoke while working on the vehicle. Before jacking up the vehicle, apply wheel chocks or other tire blocks to the wheels to prevent the vehicle from moving. After jacking up the vehicle, support the vehicle weight with safety stands at the points designated for proper lifting before working on the vehicle. These operations should be done on a level surface. When removing a heavy component such as the engine or transaxle/transmission, be careful not to lose your balance and drop them. Also, do not allow them to strike adjacent parts, especially the brake tubes and master cylinder. Before starting repairs which do not require battery power, always turn off the ignition switch, then disconnect the ground cable from the battery to prevent accidental short circuit. To prevent serious burns, avoid contact with hot metal parts such as the radiator, exhaust manifold, tail pipe and muffler. Do not remove the radiator cap when the engine is hot. Illustration of a car with exhaust fumes and ventilation warning Illustration of a person jacking up a vehicle with safety stands Illustration of a battery with ground cable being disconnected Illustration warning about hot engine parts and burns

13.title: PRECAUTIONS
13.body: PRECAUTIONS GI-3 General Precautions (continued) Before servicing the vehicle, protect fenders, upholstery and carpeting with appropriate covers. Take caution that keys, buckles or buttons on your person do not scratch the paint. Clean all disassembled parts in the designated liquid or solvent prior to inspection or assembly. Replace oil seals, gaskets, packings, O-rings, locking washers, cotter pins, self-locking nuts, etc. with new ones. Replace inner and outer races of tapered roller bearings and needle bearings as a set. Arrange the disassembled parts in accordance with their assembled locations and sequence. Do not touch the terminals of electrical components which use microcomputers (such as electronic control units). Static electricity may damage internal electronic components. After disconnecting vacuum or air hoses, attach a tag to indicate the proper connection. Use only the lubricants specified in MA section. Use approved bonding agent, sealants or their equivalents when required. Use tools and recommended special tools where specified for safe and efficient service repairs. When repairing the fuel, oil, water, vacuum or exhaust systems, check all affected lines for leaks. Dispose of drained oil or the solvent used for cleaning parts in an appropriate manner. Precautions for E.F.I. or E.C.C.S. Engine Before connecting or disconnecting E.F.I. or E.C.C.S. harness connector to or from any E.F.I. or E.C.C.S. control unit, be sure to turn the ignition switch to the "OFF" position and disconnect the negative battery terminal. Otherwise, there may be damage to control unit. Before disconnecting pressurized fuel line from fuel pump to injectors, be sure to release fuel pressure to eliminate danger. Be careful not to damage components such as control unit and air flow meter. Illustration showing a technician working on a vehicle engine bay with seat cover and fender cover in place to protect upholstery and paintwork. Illustration showing a technician receiving an electric shock/spark from a control unit, warning against touching ECU terminals without precaution.

16.title: PRECAUTIONS
16.body: PRECAUTIONS GI-4 If a large amount of unburned fuel flows into the converter, the converter temperature will be excessively high. To prevent this, follow the procedure below: Precautions for Catalyst Use unleaded gasoline only. Leaded gasoline will seriously damage the catalytic converter. When checking for ignition spark or measuring engine compression, make tests quickly and only when necessary. Do not run engine when the fuel tank level is low, otherwise the engine may misfire causing damage to the converter. Do not place the vehicle on inflammable material. Keep inflammable material off the exhaust pipe. This vehicle contains a small number of asbestos parts. The asbestos parts used on this car are minimal and will not be encountered in normal service routines. Consequently they offer no significant danger to health. Use unleaded gasoline of at least 95 octane (RON) CAUTION: Do not use leaded gasoline. Using leaded gasoline will damage the catalytic converter. Cartoon illustration of a car crash/accident scene with the caption 'Clean floor'

19.title: HOW TO USE THIS MANUAL
19.body: HOW TO USE THIS MANUAL GI-5 HOW TO USE THIS MANUAL A QUICK REFERENCE INDEX, a black tab (e.g. [BR]) is provided on the first page. You can quickly find the first page of each section by mating it to the section's black tab. THE CONTENTS are listed on the first page of each section. THE TITLE is indicated on the upper portion of each page and shows the part or system. THE PAGE NUMBER of each section consists of two letters which designate the particular section and a number (e.g. "BR-5"). THE LARGE ILLUSTRATIONS are exploded views (See below.) and contain tightening torques, lubrication points and other information necessary to perform repairs. The illustrations should be used in reference to service matters only. When ordering parts, refer to the appropriate PARTS CATALOG. THE SMALL ILLUSTRATIONS show the important steps such as inspection, use of special tools, work procedures and hidden or tricky steps which are not shown in the previous large illustrations. Assembly, inspection and adjustment procedures for the complicated units such as the automatic transaxle or transmission, etc. are presented in a step-by-step format where necessary. Pad retainer (Upper side) Torque: 54 - 64 (5.5 - 6.5, 40 - 47) N·m (kg-m, ft-lb) Torque member Pin boot Main pin Apply grease to sliding portion Inner shim Copper washer Torque: 17 - 20 (1.7 - 2.0, 12 - 14) N·m (kg-m, ft-lb) Pad retainer (Lower side) Apply grease Outer shim Pad Dust seal Apply grease Piston seal Apply grease; Replace Piston Apply grease Brake hose Air bleeder Torque: 7 - 9 (0.7 - 0.9, 5.1 - 6.5) N·m (kg-m, ft-lb) Pin bolt Torque: 22 - 31 (2.2 - 3.2, 16 - 23) N·m (kg-m, ft-lb) Cylinder body Pad retainer (Upper side) bolt torque 54 - 64 N·m also (5.5 - 6.5 kg-m, 40 - 47 ft-lb) Copper washer torque 17 - 20 N·m also (1.7 - 2.0 kg-m, 12 - 14 ft-lb) Air bleeder torque 7 - 9 N·m also (0.7 - 0.9 kg-m, 5.1 - 6.5 ft-lb) Pin bolt torque 22 - 31 N·m also (2.2 - 3.2 kg-m, 16 - 23 ft-lb) Example exploded view of a disc brake caliper assembly showing tightening torques, lubrication points and component identification callouts

25.title: HOW TO READ WIRING DIAGRAMS
25.body: HOW TO READ WIRING DIAGRAMS GI-7 Symbols used in WIRING DIAGRAM are shown below: Example wiring diagram with annotated symbols CONNECTOR — This shows that these connectors are white 6 terminal connectors. POWER SUPPLY — This shows the ignition switch position in which the system can be operated. See POWER SUPPLY ROUTING in EL section for the detailed wiring diagram. IGNITION SWITCH ACC or ON FUSE BLOCK SWITCH — This shows that continuity exists between terminals 1 and 3 when the switch is turned to ON position. The number is identical with the one in HARNESS LAYOUT. 34.title: HOW TO FOLLOW FLOW CHART IN TROUBLE DIAGNOSES
34.body:
HOW TO FOLLOW FLOW CHART IN TROUBLE DIAGNOSES GI-10 Example Flow Chart — Crankangle Sensor Power and Ground Check INSPECTION START CHECK POWER SUPPLY.
1) Turn ignition switch "ON".
2) Check voltage between terminal (b) and ground.
Battery voltage should exist. Check the following items.
1) Harness continuity between crankangle sensor and battery
2) E.C.C.S. relay-1 (See page EF & EC-106.)
3) "BR" fusible link
4) Power source for E.C.U. (See page EF & EC-104.)
5) Ignition switch CHECK GROUND CIRCUIT.
1) Turn ignition switch "OFF".
2) Disconnect crankangle sensor harness connector.
3) Check resistance between terminal (d) and ground.
Resistance: Approximately 0Ω Check the following items.
1) Harness continuity between crankangle sensor and ground
2) Ground circuit for E.C.U. (See page EF & EC-104.) O.K. (proceed) N.G. O.K. N.G. O.K. The flow chart indicates work procedures required to diagnose problems effectively. Observe the following instructions before diagnosing. NOTICE — Instructions for Using Flow Chart Use the flow chart after locating probable causes of a problem following the "Preliminary Check" or the "Symptom Chart". After repairs, re-check that the problem has been completely eliminated. Refer to Component Parts Location and Harness Layout for the Systems described in each section for identification/location of components and harness connectors. Refer to the Circuit Diagram for Quick Pinpoint Check. If you must check circuit continuity between harness connectors in more detail, such as when a sub-harness is used, refer to Wiring Diagram and Harness Layout in EL section for identification of harness connectors. When checking circuit continuity, ignition switch should be "OFF". Before checking voltage at connectors, check battery voltage. After accomplishing the Diagnostic Procedures and Electrical Components Inspection, make sure that all harness connectors are reconnected as they were. Example diagram A — showing voltmeter connected to crankangle sensor connector (CONNECT position), checking voltage between terminal (b) and ground with ignition ON. Example diagram B — showing ohmmeter connected to crankangle sensor connector (DISCONNECT position), checking resistance between terminal (d) and ground with ignition OFF.

37.title: HOW TO FOLLOW FLOW CHART IN TROUBLE DIAGNOSES
37.body:
HOW TO FOLLOW FLOW CHART IN TROUBLE DIAGNOSES GI-11 Start to diagnose a problem using procedures indicated in enclosed blocks, as shown in the following example. Example flow chart block A (entry point) CHECK POWER SUPPLY.
1) Turn ignition switch "ON".
2) Check voltage between terminal (b) and ground.
Battery voltage should exist. O.K. O.K. Required results are indicated in bold type in the corresponding block, as shown below:
These have the following meanings:
Battery voltage → 11 - 14V
Voltage: Approximately 0V → Less than 1V Illustrations are provided as visual aids for work procedures. For example, symbol A indicated in the left upper portion of each illustration corresponds with the symbol in the flow chart for easy identification. More precisely, the procedure under the "CHECK POWER SUPPLY" outlined previously is indicated by an illustration A. Symbols included in illustrations refer to measurements or procedures. Before diagnosing a problem, familiarize yourself with each symbol. Example flow chart block showing CHECK POWER SUPPLY step with annotations pointing to 'Check item being performed' and 'Procedure, steps or measurement results', with O.K. output arrow

43.title: HOW TO FOLLOW FLOW CHART IN TROUBLE DIAGNOSES
43.body: HOW TO FOLLOW FLOW CHART IN TROUBLE DIAGNOSES GI-13 Key to symbols signifying measurements or procedures Symbol Symbol explanation Symbol Symbol explanation [DISCONNECT icon] Check after disconnecting the connector to be measured. [ammeter icon] Current should be measured with an ammeter [CONNECT icon] Check after connecting the connector to be measured. [CONSULT icon] Procedure with CONSULT [key insert icon] Insert key into ignition switch. [no CONSULT icon] Procedure without CONSULT [key remove icon] Remove key from ignition switch. [A/C switch OFF icon] A/C switch is "OFF". [ignition OFF icon] Turn ignition switch to "OFF" position. [A/C switch ON icon] A/C switch is "ON". [ignition ON icon] Turn ignition switch to "ON" position. [fan switch ON icon] Fan switch is "ON". (At any position except for "OFF" position) [ignition START icon] Turn ignition switch to "START" position. [fan switch OFF icon] Fan switch is "OFF". [OFF to ACC icon] Turn ignition switch from "OFF" to "ACC" position. [battery voltage icon] Apply battery voltage directly to components. [ACC to OFF icon] Turn ignition switch from "ACC" to "OFF" position. [drive vehicle icon] Drive vehicle. [OFF to ON icon] Turn ignition switch from "OFF" to "ON" position. [battery disconnect icon] Disconnect battery negative cable. [ON to OFF icon] Turn ignition switch from "ON" to "OFF" position. [brake depress icon] Depress brake pedal. [engine stopped icon] Do not start engine, or check with engine stopped. [brake release icon] Release brake pedal. [engine running icon] Start engine, or check with engine running. [accelerator depress icon] Depress accelerator pedal. [parking brake apply icon] Apply parking brake. [accelerator release icon] Release accelerator pedal. [parking brake release icon] Release parking brake. [SMJ connector diagram] Pin terminal check for S.M.J. type E.C.U. and A/T control unit connectors. For details regarding the terminal arrangement, refer to the foldout page. [engine warm icon] Check after engine is warmed up sufficiently.   [voltmeter icon] Voltage should be measured with a voltmeter.   [ohmmeter icon] Circuit resistance should be measured with an ohmmeter. 45.body: HOW TO FOLLOW FLOW CHART IN TROUBLE DIAGNOSES GI-13 Key to symbols signifying measurements or procedures Symbol Symbol explanation DISCONNECT (connector icon) Check after disconnecting the connector to be measured. CONNECT (connector icon) Check after connecting the connector to be measured. Key insert icon Insert key into ignition switch. Key remove icon Remove key from ignition switch. Ignition OFF icon Turn ignition switch to "OFF" position. Ignition ON icon Turn ignition switch to "ON" position. Ignition START icon Turn ignition switch to "START" position. OFF to ACC icon Turn ignition switch from "OFF" to "ACC" position. ACC to OFF icon Turn ignition switch from "ACC" to "OFF" position. OFF to ON icon Turn ignition switch from "OFF" to "ON" position. ON to OFF icon Turn ignition switch from "ON" to "OFF" position. Engine stopped icon Do not start engine, or check with engine stopped. Engine running icon Start engine, or check with engine running. Parking brake apply icon Apply parking brake. Parking brake release icon Release parking brake. Engine warm icon (C to H) Check after engine is warmed up sufficiently. Voltmeter icon Voltage should be measured with a voltmeter. Ohmmeter icon Circuit resistance should be measured with an ohmmeter. Ammeter icon Current should be measured with an ammeter. CONSULT icon Procedure with CONSULT. No CONSULT icon Procedure without CONSULT. A/C switch OFF icon A/C switch is "OFF". A/C switch ON icon A/C switch is "ON". Fan switch ON icon (OFF 1 2 3 4) Fan switch is "ON". (At any position except for "OFF" position) Fan switch OFF icon (OFF 1 2 3 4) Fan switch is "OFF". Battery/component icon Apply battery voltage directly to components. Drive vehicle icon Drive vehicle. Battery negative cable icon Disconnect battery negative cable. Brake pedal depress icon Depress brake pedal. Brake pedal release icon Release brake pedal. Accelerator pedal depress icon Depress accelerator pedal. Accelerator pedal release icon Release accelerator pedal. SMJ/ECU connector pin terminal icon Pin terminal check for S.M.J. type E.C.U. and A/T control unit connectors. For details regarding the terminal arrangement, refer to the foldout page.

49.title: CONSULT CHECKING SYSTEM
49.body: CONSULT CHECKING SYSTEM GI-15 Function Diagnostic mode Function Work support This mode enables a technician to adjust some devices faster and more accurately by following the indications on CONSULT. Self-diagnostic results Self-diagnostic results can be read and erased quickly. Data monitor Input/Output data in the control unit can be read. Active test Mode in which CONSULT drives some actuators apart from the control units and also shifts some parameters in a specified range. E.C.U. part number E.C.U. part number can be read. Function test Allows engine circuits to be tested sequentially or individually from a test menu When ordering the below equipment, contact your NISSAN distributor. Checking Equipment Tool name Description NISSAN CONSULT kit  (1) CONSULT unit and accessories Main CONSULT diagnostic unit with accessories (2) Program card Program card for CONSULT unit (3) Operation manuals Operation manuals for CONSULT system (4) Binder Binder for documentation (5) Carrying case Carrying case for CONSULT kit (6) Thermal paper (Rolls) Thermal paper rolls for CONSULT printer Illustration of NISSAN CONSULT kit components: (1) CONSULT unit and accessories, (2) Program card, (3) Operation manuals, (4) Binder, (5) Carrying case, (6) Thermal paper (Rolls)

Position 5: B = L grade, D = LX grade, F = SLX grade, T = SUPER S grade. Position 6: F = 5-speed manual transaxle, Z = N-CVT. Fixed segment: K11. Suffix 1: E = E.G.I. 70.body: LIFTING POINTS AND TOW TRUCK TOWING GI-22 Make sure vehicle is empty when lifting. 73.title: LIFTING POINTS AND TOW TRUCK TOWING — Garage Jack and Safety Stand
73.body: LIFTING POINTS AND TOW TRUCK TOWING — Garage Jack and Safety Stand GI-23 Never get under the vehicle while it is supported only by the jack. Always use safety stands when you have to get under the vehicle. 76.title: LIFTING POINTS AND TOW TRUCK TOWING
76.body:
LIFTING POINTS AND TOW TRUCK TOWING GI-24 When lifting the vehicle, open the lift arms as wide as possible and ensure that the front and rear of the vehicle are well balanced.
When setting the lift arm, do not allow the arm to contact the brake tubes and fuel lines. Underside view of vehicle showing 2-pole lift points with 850mm spacing, sill, lift pad, and center of gravity Front 850mm Sill Lift pad Center of gravity Note.
Lift-up points are the same as pantograph jack points. Put the sill in the slit of the lift pad to prevent the sill from deforming. If the pad does not have a slit, prepare a suitable attachment with one. Underside view of vehicle showing 2-pole lift points, sill locations, center of gravity, lift pad detail, and 850mm dimension between lift points

79.title: LIFTING POINTS AND TOW TRUCK TOWING
79.body:
LIFTING POINTS AND TOW TRUCK TOWING GI-25 All applicable local laws regarding the towing operation must be obeyed.
It is necessary to use proper towing equipment to avoid possible damage to the vehicle during a towing operation.
When towing with the rear wheels on the ground, release the parking brake and move the gearshift lever to neutral ("N" position). NISSAN recommends that vehicles be towed with the driving (front) wheels off the ground as illustrated. Before towing an N-CVT model with four wheels on ground, the N-CVT fluid level should be checked.
Observe the following restricted towing speeds and distances. Speed Below 30 km/h (20 MPH) N-CVT model with four wheels on ground Distance Less than 30 km (20 miles) N-CVT model with four wheels on ground Never tow an N-CVT model from the rear (i.e., backward) with four wheels on the ground as this may cause serious and expensive damage to the transaxle. Never tow an N-CVT model with rear wheels raised (with front wheels on ground) as this may cause serious and expensive damage to the transaxle. If it is necessary to tow it with rear wheels raised, always use a towing dolly under the front wheels. N-CVT (Nissan Continuously Variable Transmission) specific towing restrictions noted. Different procedures apply for N-CVT vs standard manual transmission models. Two illustrations showing recommended tow truck towing method with front (driving) wheels off the ground — one hatchback, one sedan Illustration showing incorrect towing of N-CVT model with rear wheels raised and front wheels on ground

80.title: LIFTING POINTS AND TOW TRUCK TOWING
80.body: LIFTING POINTS AND TOW TRUCK TOWING GI-25 All applicable local laws regarding the towing operation must be obeyed. It is necessary to use proper towing equipment to avoid possible damage to the vehicle during a towing operation. When towing with the rear wheels on the ground, release the parking brake and move the gearshift lever to neutral ("N" position). NISSAN recommends that vehicles be towed with the driving (front) wheels off the ground as illustrated. Before towing an N-CVT model with four wheels on ground, the N-CVT fluid level should be checked. Observe the following restricted towing speeds and distances. Speed Below 30 km/h (20 MPH) N-CVT model, four wheels on ground Distance Less than 30 km (20 miles) N-CVT model, four wheels on ground Never tow an N-CVT model from the rear (i.e., backward) with four wheels on the ground as this may cause serious and expensive damage to the transaxle. Never tow an N-CVT model with rear wheels raised (with front wheels on ground) as this may cause serious and expensive damage to the transaxle. If it is necessary to tow it with rear wheels raised, always use a towing dolly under the front wheels. N-CVT model specific towing restrictions apply. M/T models may be towed with rear wheels on ground provided parking brake is released and gearshift is in neutral. Two illustrations showing NISSAN recommended towing method with driving (front) wheels off the ground — both flatbed and wheel-lift tow truck configurations shown Illustration showing incorrect towing of N-CVT model with rear wheels raised and front wheels on ground, with shading indicating prohibited method

81.title: LIFTING POINTS AND TOW TRUCK TOWING
81.body: LIFTING POINTS AND TOW TRUCK TOWING GI-25 All applicable local laws regarding the towing operation must be obeyed. It is necessary to use proper towing equipment to avoid possible damage to the vehicle during a towing operation. When towing with the rear wheels on the ground, release the parking brake and move the gearshift lever to neutral ("N" position). NISSAN recommends that vehicles be towed with the driving (front) wheels off the ground as illustrated. Before towing an N-CVT model with four wheels on ground, the N-CVT fluid level should be checked. Observe the following restricted towing speeds and distances. Speed Below 30 km/h (20 MPH) N-CVT model with four wheels on ground Distance Less than 30 km (20 miles) N-CVT model with four wheels on ground Never tow an N-CVT model from the rear (i.e., backward) with four wheels on the ground as this may cause serious and expensive damage to the transaxle. Never tow an N-CVT model with rear wheels raised (with front wheels on ground) as this may cause serious and expensive damage to the transaxle. If it is necessary to tow it with rear wheels raised, always use a towing dolly under the front wheels. N-CVT model specific towing restrictions apply. Four-wheel-on-ground towing speed limited to below 30 km/h (20 MPH) and distance less than 30 km (20 miles). Rear-wheel-raised towing requires towing dolly under front wheels. Two illustrations showing tow truck towing a vehicle: top image shows front wheels off the ground (recommended), bottom image shows an alternative towing position Illustration showing N-CVT model being towed with rear wheels raised and front wheels on ground — incorrect method warning illustration

82.title: LIFTING POINTS AND TOW TRUCK TOWING
82.body: LIFTING POINTS AND TOW TRUCK TOWING GI-26 Always pull the cable straight out from the vehicle. Never pull on the hook at a sideways angle. 107.title: Precautions for Fuel
107.body: Precautions for Fuel GI-3002 Unleaded gasoline of at least 91 octane (RON) Do not use leaded gasoline. Using leaded gasoline will damage the catalytic converter. Australia market specification only: unleaded gasoline minimum 91 octane (RON). Leaded gasoline prohibited.

108.title: MODIFICATIONS
108.body: MODIFICATIONS GI-3003 The K11 model has been adopted for Australia. The ECM ROM data has been modified for the use of 91 octane fuel. Child Restraint anchoring points have been introduced. K11 model adopted for Australia. ECM ROM modified for 91 octane fuel. Child Restraint anchoring points introduced.

113.title: MODIFICATIONS
113.body: MODIFICATIONS GI-4002 Introduction of NATS V.2.0. Immobiliser System. Introduction of Seat Belt Pre-tensioners on the front seats. Introduction of a child restraint fixing function (A/ELR) for the rear outer 3-point seat belts. Various interior trim changes. Introduction of Combi Switch with integrated Fog Lamp Switch. Introduction of Logical Lighting.

114.title: MODIFICATIONS
114.body: MODIFICATIONS GI-4002 Introduction of NATS V.2.0. Immobiliser System. Introduction of Seat Belt Pre-tensioners on the front seats. Introduction of a child restraint fixing function (A/ELR) for the rear outer 3-point seat belts. Various interior trim changes. Introduction of Combi Switch with integrated Fog Lamp Switch. Introduction of Logical Lighting.

119.title: Supplemental Restraint System (SRS) "AIR BAG" and "SEAT BELT PRE-TENSIONER" (Dual Air Bag System)
119.body: Supplemental Restraint System (SRS) "AIR BAG" and "SEAT BELT PRE-TENSIONER" (Dual Air Bag System) GI-5002 The Supplemental Restraint System "Air Bag" and "Seat Belt Pre-tensioner", used along with a seat belt, help to reduce the risk or severity of injury to the driver and front passenger in a frontal collision. The Supplemental Restraint System consists of air bag modules (located in the center of the steering wheel and on the instrument panel on the passenger side), seat belt pre-tensioners, a diagnosis sensor unit, warning lamp, wiring harness and spiral cable. To avoid rendering the SRS inoperative, which could increase the risk of personal injury or death (in the event of a collision which would result in air bag inflation), all maintenance must be performed by an authorized NISSAN dealer. Improper maintenance, including incorrect removal and installation of the SRS, can lead to personal injury caused by unintentional activation of the system. Do not use electrical test equipment on any circuit related to the SRS unless instructed to do so in this Service Manual. SRS wiring harnesses are covered with yellow insulation (either just before the harness connectors or for the complete harness), for easy identification.

121.title: Precautions for Fuel
121.body: Precautions for Fuel GI-5003 Diesel fuel of at least 50 cetane * If two types of diesel fuel are available, use summer or winter fuel properly according to the following temperature conditions. Summer type diesel fuel Above -7°C (20°F) Winter type diesel fuel Below -7°C (20°F) Do not use home heating oil, gasoline, or other alternate fuels in your diesel engine. The use of fuels other than those recommended by the manufacturer can cause engine damage. Do not use summer fuel at temperatures below -7°C (20°F). Cold temperatures will cause wax to form in the fuel, as a result, it may prevent the engine from running smoothly. Do not add gasoline or other alternative fuels to diesel fuel. Diesel engine variant only (marked with asterisk)

122.title: Precautions for Fuel
122.body: Precautions for Fuel GI-5003 Diesel fuel of at least 50 cetane If two types of diesel fuel are available, use summer or winter fuel properly according to the following temperature conditions. Above -7°C (20°F) ... Summer type diesel fuel. Below -7°C (20°F) ... Winter type diesel fuel. Do not use home heating oil, gasoline, or other alternate fuels in your diesel engine. The use of fuels other than those recommended by the manufacturer can cause engine damage. Do not use summer fuel at temperatures below -7°C (20°F). Cold temperatures will cause wax to form in the fuel, as a result, it may prevent the engine from running smoothly. Do not add gasoline or other alternative fuels to diesel fuel. Diesel engine variant only (marked with asterisk *)

135.title: Supplemental Restraint System (SRS) "AIR BAG" and "SEAT BELT PRE-TENSIONER"
135.body: Supplemental Restraint System (SRS) "AIR BAG" and "SEAT BELT PRE-TENSIONER" GI-6002 The Supplemental Restraint System "Air Bag" and "Seat Belt Pre-tensioner", used along with a seat belt, help to reduce the risk or severity of injury to the driver and front passenger in a frontal collision. The Supplemental Restraint System consists of air bag modules (located in the center of the steering wheel and on the instrument panel on the passenger side), seat belt pre-tensioners, a diagnosis sensor unit, warning lamp, wiring harness and spiral cable. If the vehicle is equipped with side air bag as the Supplemental Restraint System, the side air bag used along with the seat belt helps to reduce the risk or severity of injury to the driver and front passenger in a side collision. The side air bag consists of air bag modules (located in the outer side of front seats), satellite sensor, diagnosis sensor unit (which is one of components of air bags for a frontal collision), wiring harness, warning lamp (which is one of components of air bags for a frontal collision). WARNING: To avoid rendering the SRS inoperative, which could increase the risk of personal injury or death (in the event of a collision which would result in air bag inflation), all maintenance must be performed by an authorized NISSAN dealer. Improper maintenance, including incorrect removal and installation of the SRS, can lead to personal injury caused by unintentional activation of the system. Do not use electrical test equipment on any circuit related to the SRS unless instructed to do so in this Service Manual. SRS wiring harnesses are covered with yellow insulation (either just before the harness connectors or for the complete harness), for easy identification.

140.title: CONSULT-II CHECKING SYSTEM
140.body:
CONSULT-II CHECKING SYSTEM GI-7003 When ordering the below equipment, contact your NISSAN distributor. Checking Equipment Tool name Description NISSAN CONSULT-II
① CONSULT-II unit and accessories
② Program card AED00A and AEN00A (For NATS)
To confirm the best combination of tester internal software and the above mentioned software, refer to CONSULT-II Operation Manual. [See figure SGI083A] NOTE:
• The CONSULT-II must be used in conjunction with a program card. CONSULT-II does not require loading (Initialization) procedure.
• Be sure the CONSULT-II is turned off before installing or removing a program card. CONSULT-II Data Link Connector location in vehicle DDL-2 connector (Petrol & Diesel engine models) — Petrol: all available systems — Diesel: ABS, airbag DDL-1 connector (Diesel engine models only) Diesel: NATS If the CONSULT-II cannot diagnose the system properly, check the following items. Inspection Procedure Symptom Check item CONSULT-II cannot access any system. • CONSULT-II DLC power supply circuit (Terminal 8) and ground circuit (Terminal 4) (For detailed circuit, refer to EF&EC-7251, "MI & Data Link Connectors Wiring Diagram".)
• CONSULT-II DDL cable CONSULT-II cannot access individual system. (Other systems can be accessed.) • CONSULT-II program card (Check the approprite CONSULT-II program card for the system. Refer to "Checking Equipment" above.)
• Power supply and ground circuit for the control unite of the system (For detailed circuit, refer to wiring diagram for each system.)
• Open or short circuit between the system and CONSULT-II DLC (For detailed circuit, refer to wiring diagram for each system.) DDL-2 connector: Petrol & Diesel engine models (Petrol: all available systems; Diesel: ABS, airbag). DDL-1 connector: Diesel engine models only (Diesel: NATS). NISSAN CONSULT-II unit (item 1) and program card (item 2) CONSULT-II Data Link Connector location showing DDL-2 connector (Petrol & Diesel engine models) and DDL-1 connector (Diesel engine models only)

141.title: CONSULT-II CHECKING SYSTEM
141.body:
CONSULT-II CHECKING SYSTEM GI-7004 Function and System Application Diagnostic test mode Function ENGINE A/T ABS AIR BAG NATS* Work support This mode enables a technician to adjust some devices faster and more accurate by following the indications on CONSULT-II. x — — — — Self-diagnostic results Self-diagnostic results can be read and erased quickly. x x x x x Trouble diagnostic record Current self-diagnostic results and all trouble diagnostic records previously stored can be read. — — — x — ECU discriminated No. Classification number of a replacement ECU can be read to prevent an incorrect ECU from being installed. — — — x — Data monitor Input/Output data in the ECU (ECM) can be read. x x x — — DTC work support This mode enables a technician to set operating conditions to confirm self-diagnoses status/results. — x — — — Active test Diagnostic Test Mode in which CONSULT-II drives some actuators apart from the ECMs and also shifts some parameters in a specified range. x — x — — ECU (ECM) part number ECU (ECM) part number can be read. x x x — — Control unit initialization All registered ignition key IDs in NATS components can be initialized and new IDs can be registered. — — — — — x : Applicable
* : NATS (Nissan Anti-Theft System) CONSULT-II contains a nickel metal hydride battery. When replacing the battery obey the following: Replace the nickel metal hydride battery with Genuine CONSULT-II battery only. Use of another battery may present a risk of fire or explosion. The battery may present a fire or chemical burn hazard if mistreated. Do not recharge, disassemble of dispose of in fire.
Keep the battery out of reach of children and discard used battery conforming to the local regulations.

149.title: PRE-DELIVERY INSPECTION ITEMS
149.body: PRE-DELIVERY INSPECTION ITEMS MA-3 Shown below are Pre-delivery Inspection Items required for the new vehicle. It is recommended that necessary items other than those listed here be added, paying due regard to the conditions in each country. Perform applicable items on each model. Consult text of this section for specifications. UNDER HOOD — engine off Radiator coolant level and coolant hose connections for leaks Battery fluid level, specific gravity and condition of battery terminals Drive belts tension Fuel lines and connections for leaks Engine oil level and oil leaks Brake reservoir fluid level and fluid lines for leaks Windshield and rear window washer and headlamp cleaner reservoir fluid level Power steering reservoir fluid level and hose connections for leaks INSIDE AND OUTSIDE Remove front spring/strut spacer (If applicable) Operation of all instruments, gauges, lights and accessories Operation of horn(s), wiper and washer Steering lock for operation Air conditioner for gas leaks Front and rear seats, and seat belts for operation All moldings, trims and fittings for fit and alignment All windows for operation and alignment Hood, trunk lid, door panels for fit and alignment Latches, keys and locks for operation Weatherstrips for adhesion and fit Headlamp aiming Tighten wheel nuts Tire pressure (Inc. spare tire) Check front wheels for toe-in Install clock/voltmeter/room lamp fuse (If applicable) Remove wiper blade protectors (If applicable) UNDER BODY Manual transmission/transaxle, transfer and differential gear oil level Brake and fuel lines and oil/fluid reservoirs for leaks Tighten bolts and nuts of steering linkage and gearbox, suspension, and drive shafts ROAD TEST Clutch operation Parking brake operation Service brake operation Automatic transmission/transaxle shift timing and kickdown Steering control and returnability Engine performance Squeaks and rattles ENGINE OPERATING AND HOT Automatic transmission/transaxle fluid level Engine idling FINAL INSPECTION Install necessary parts (outside mirror, wheel covers, seat belts, mat, carpet or mud flaps) Inspect for interior and exterior metal and paint damage Check for spare tire, jack, tools and literature Wash, clean interior and exterior Items marked 'If applicable' may not apply to all variants. Automatic transmission items apply only to A/T equipped models. Manual transmission items apply only to M/T equipped models.

150.title: GENERAL MAINTENANCE
150.body: GENERAL MAINTENANCE MA-4 General maintenance includes those items which should be checked during the normal day-to-day operation of the vehicle. They are essential if the vehicle is to continue operating properly. The owners can perform the checks and inspections themselves or they can have their NISSAN dealers do them. General Maintenance Items Item Reference pages OUTSIDE THE VEHICLE  The maintenance items listed here should be performed from time to time, unless otherwise specified.  Tires Check the pressure with a gauge periodically when at a service station, including the spare, and adjust to the specified pressure if necessary. Check carefully for damage, cuts or excessive wear. — Windshield wiper blades Check for cracks or wear if they do not wipe properly. — Doors and engine hood Check that all doors, the engine hood, the trunk lid and back door operate properly. Also ensure that all latches lock securely. Lubricate if necessary. Make sure that the secondary latch keeps the hood from opening when the primary latch is released. When driving in areas using road salt or other corrosive materials, check for lubrication frequently. MA-23 Tire rotation Tires should be rotated every 10,000 km (6,000 miles). MA-22 INSIDE THE VEHICLE  The maintenance items listed here should be checked on a regular basis, such as when performing periodic maintenance, cleaning the vehicle, etc.  Lights Make sure that the headlights, stop lights, tail lights, turn signal lights, and other lights are all operating properly and installed securely. Also check headlight aim. — Warning lights and chimes Make sure that all warning lights and chimes are operating properly. — Steering wheel Check for change in the steering conditions, such as excessive free play, hard steering or strange noises. Free play: Less than 35 mm (1.38 in) ST-4 UNDER THE HOOD AND VEHICLE  The maintenance items listed here should be checked periodically e.g. each time you check the engine oil or refuel.  Windshield washer fluid Check that there is adequate fluid in the tank. — Engine coolant level Check the coolant level when the engine is cold. MA-9 Engine oil level Check the level after parking the vehicle on a level spot and turning off the engine. MA-12 Brake fluid level Make sure that the brake fluid level is between the "MAX" and "MIN" lines on the reservoir. MA-19 Battery Check the fluid level in each cell. It should be between the "MAX" and "MIN" lines. EL-16 Steering wheel free play Less than 35 mm (1.38 in) Tire rotation interval Every 10,000 km (6,000 miles)

151.title: Periodic Maintenance — Maintenance Under Severe Driving Conditions
151.body:
Periodic Maintenance — Maintenance Under Severe Driving Conditions MA-6 The maintenance intervals shown on the preceding pages are for normal operating conditions. If the vehicle is mainly operated under severe driving conditions as shown below, more frequent maintenance must be performed on the following items as shown in the table. A — Driving under dusty conditions
B — Driving repeatedly short distances
C — Towing a trailer
D — Extensive idling
E — Driving in extremely adverse weather conditions or in areas where ambient temperatures are either extremely low or extremely high
F — Driving in high humidity areas or in mountainous areas
G — Driving in areas using salt or other corrosive materials
H — Driving on rough and/or muddy roads or in the desert
I — Driving with frequent use of braking or in mountainous areas Maintenance operation: Check = Check and correct or replace if necessary. Maintenance Under Severe Driving Conditions Driving condition Maintenance item Maintenance operation Maintenance interval Engine oil service A B C D Engine oil Replace Every 4,500 miles (7,500 km) or 6 months A B C D Engine oil filter Replace Every oil change Major service A Air cleaner filter & P.C.V. filter Replace Every 12 months or 18,000 miles (30,000 km) A . . . E Fuel filter Replace Every 12 months or 18,000 miles (30,000 km) . . . . . F Brake fluid Replace Every 12 months or 18,000 miles (30,000 km) . . . . . G H Steering gear & linkage, axle & suspension parts, exhaust system Check Every 12 months or 18,000 miles (30,000 km) . . C . . . H N-CVT fluid Replace Every 13,500 miles (22,500 km) . . C . . . H Manual transaxle oil Replace Every 24 months or 36,000 miles (60,000 km) A . C . . . G H I Brake pads, discs & other brake components Check Every 6 months or 9,000 miles (15,000 km) A . C . . . G H I Brake linings, drums & other brake components Check Every 6 months or 9,000 miles (15,000 km) . . C . . . H Drive shafts Check Every 6 months or 9,000 miles (15,000 km) N-CVT fluid entry applies to CVT-equipped models; Manual transaxle oil entry applies to M/T-equipped models.

153.title: Checking Drive Belts
153.body: Checking Drive Belts MA-8 Checking Drive Belts Inspect drive belts for cracks, fraying, wear or oil adhesion. If necessary, replace with a new one. Inspect drive belt deflections by pushing on the belt midway between pulleys. Inspect drive belt deflections when the engine is cold. Adjust if belt deflections exceed the limit. Drive Belt Deflection Specifications Configuration Belt Limit for adjustment When adjusted Deflection of new belt Non-Powersteering and Non-Airconditioning Alternator belt 10.5 (0.413) 7 - 8 (0.28 - 0.31) 6 - 7 (0.24 - 0.28) Non-Powersteering and Non-Airconditioning Water-pump belt 6 (0.24) 3.8 - 4.2 (0.150 - 0.165) 3.5 - 4 (0.138 - 0.157) Powersteering and Non-Airconditioning Alternator belt 9 (0.35) 6 - 7 (0.24 - 0.28) 5.5 - 6 (0.217 - 0.236) Powersteering and Non-Airconditioning Water-pump belt 5 (0.20) 3 - 3.5 (0.118 - 0.138) 2.8 - 3.2 (0.110 - 0.126) Airconditioning and Non-Powersteering Alternator belt 9 (0.35) 6 - 7 (0.24 - 0.28) 5.5 - 6 (0.217 - 0.236) Airconditioning and Non-Powersteering Water-pump belt 6 (0.24) 3.8 - 4.2 (0.150 - 0.165) 3.5 - 4 (0.138 - 0.157) Airconditioning and Powersteering Alternator belt 9 (0.35) 6 - 7 (0.24 - 0.28) 5.5 - 6 (0.217 - 0.236) Airconditioning and Powersteering Water-pump belt 5 (0.20) 3 - 3.5 (0.118 - 0.138) 2.8 - 3.2 (0.110 - 0.126) Applied pushing force: 98N (10 kg, 22 lb) All models: Adjustment of waterpump drive belt Remove splash guards. Slacken tensioner idler locking nut from below. Move tensioning idler by turning adjusting bolt from below, until desired belt deflection is obtained. Tighten tensioner idler locking nut. Install splash guards. Models without powersteering and without airconditioning: Adjustment of alternator drive belt Release all three alternator fixing bolts. Tilt alternator by turning the adjusting bolt until desired belt deflection is obtained. Tighten the alternator fixing bolts to specified torque. 38.0 - 50.9 N·m (3.87 - 5.19 kg-m, 28.0 - 37.5 ft-lb) Alternator fixing bolts torque 38.0 - 50.9 N·m Models without powersteering and without airconditioning Alternator fixing bolts torque 3.87 - 5.19 kg-m Models without powersteering and without airconditioning Alternator fixing bolts torque 28.0 - 37.5 ft-lb Models without powersteering and without airconditioning Models with powersteering or airconditioning, or both: Adjustment of alternator drive belt Remove splash guards. Slacken tensioner idler locking nut from below. Move tensioning idler by turning adjusting bolt with a 12 mm socket from top, until desired belt deflection is obtained. Tighten tensioning idler locking nut. Install splash guards. Four belt routing configurations covered: Non-Powersteering/Non-Airconditioning, Powersteering/Non-Airconditioning, Airconditioning/Non-Powersteering, Airconditioning and Powersteering. Unit: mm (in) for all deflection values. Belt routing diagram for Non-Powersteering and Non-Airconditioning configuration showing Water pump, Alternator, Crankshaft, Tensioning idler Belt routing diagram for Powersteering and Non-Airconditioning configuration showing Water pump, Tensioning idler, Alternator, Power steering pump, Fixed idler, Crankshaft Belt routing diagram for Airconditioning and Non-Powersteering configuration showing Water pump, Tensioning idler, Alternator, Crankshaft, Aircon-compressor, Tensioning idler Belt routing diagram for Airconditioning and Powersteering configuration showing Water pump, Tensioning idler, Alternator, Power steering pump, Crankshaft, Aircon-compressor, Tensioning idler Photo showing adjusting bolt, locking nut locations for tensioning idler and alternator belt adjustment

154.title: Changing Engine Coolant
154.body: Changing Engine Coolant MA-9 WARNING: To avoid being scalded, never change the coolant when the engine is hot. Changing Engine Coolant Turn heater "TEMP" control all the way to "HOT". Remove radiator cap. Disconnect lower radiator hose to drain coolant. Remove reservoir tank, drain coolant, then clean reservoir tank. Re-install it temporarily. Be careful not to allow coolant to contact drive belts. Connect lower radiator hose. Fill radiator with water up to the radiator inlet. Fill reservoir tank with water up to its "MAX" line. While keeping the radiator cap open, warm up the engine. Confirm thermostat is open by touching the radiator upper hose. Race engine 2 or 3 times under no-load. Make sure that airconditioner switch is "OFF". Stop the engine and wait until it cools down. Repeat steps 3 through 8 until clear water begins to drain from radiator. Reinstall reservoir tank. Fill radiator and reservoir tank with coolant up to specified level following steps 5 through 7. When air is released from the engine and the coolant level in the radiator has lowered, fill radiator up to the inlet. After the engine has cooled down, fill the reservoir with coolant up to its "MAX" line. Start the engine and bring engine speed up to 3,000 rpm. Confirm that water flowing through the heater core is not audible at instrument panel lower side. If it is audible, repeat steps 11 through 13. Heater control panel showing TEMP control turned all the way to HOT position Engine bay showing radiator filler cap location Underside view showing lower radiator hose location for draining coolant Cross-section diagram of radiator showing coolant inlet fill level Reservoir tank showing MAX and MIN level markings

155.title: Engine Maintenance — Changing Engine Coolant (Cont'd) / Checking Cooling System
155.body: Engine Maintenance — Changing Engine Coolant (Cont'd) / Checking Cooling System MA-10 Make sure that anti-freeze is ethylene glycol based and that the mixture ratio with (soft) water is correct. Anti-freeze / Soft Water Mixture Ratio Outside temperature down to (°C) Outside temperature down to (°F) Anti-freeze Soft water -15 5 30% 70% -35 -30 50% 50% Coolant capacity (With reservoir tank) 4 liters (7 Imp pt) liters / Imp pt With reservoir tank Check hoses for improper attachment and for leaks, cracks, damage, loose connections, chafing and deterioration. Checking Radiator Cap Apply pressure to radiator cap with cap tester to see if it is satisfactory. Pull the negative-pressure valve to open it. Check that it closes completely when released. Radiator cap relief pressure 78 - 98 kPa (0.78 - 0.98 bar, 0.8 - 1.0 kg/cm², 11 - 14 psi) kPa / bar / kg/cm² / psi Checking Cooling System for Leaks Apply pressure to the cooling system with cap tester to check for leakage. Testing pressure 98 kPa (0.98 bar, 1.0 kg/cm², 14 psi) kPa / bar / kg/cm² / psi Higher pressure than the specified value may cause damage to radiator. Radiator cap being tested with a cap tester (pressure tester applied to radiator cap) Pulling the negative-pressure valve on the radiator cap to check it closes completely when released Hose adapter connected to cooling system with cap tester to check for leaks (EG17650301 referenced)

156.title: ENGINE MAINTENANCE
156.body:
ENGINE MAINTENANCE MA-11 Inspect fuel lines and tank for improper attachment and for leaks, cracks, damage, loose connections, chafing and deterioration.
If necessary, repair or replace faulty parts. CAUTION:
Tighten high-pressure rubber hose clamp so that clamp end is 3 mm (0.12 in) from hose end.
Ensure that screw does not contact adjacent parts. WARNING:
Before removing fuel filter, release fuel pressure from fuel line to eliminate danger. Fuel Pressure Release (via CONSULT) Perform "FUEL PRESSURE RELEASE" in "WORK SUPPORT" mode and release fuel pressure to zero. Turn ignition switch off. Fuel Pressure Release (Manual Method) Remove fuse for fuel pump. Start engine. After engine stalls, crank engine two or three times to make sure that fuel pressure is released. Turn ignition switch off and install fuse for fuel pump. Fuel Filter Replacement Loosen fuel hose clamps. Replace fuel filter. Be careful not to spill fuel over engine compartment. Place a shop towel to absorb fuel. Use a high-pressure type fuel filter. Do not use a synthetic resin fuel filter. When tightening fuel hose clamps, refer to "Checking Fuel Lines". Fuse box location differs between left-hand drive and right-hand drive vehicles. Fuel pump fuse location shown for both variants. Underside vehicle diagram showing engine, fuel line, and fuel tank locations High-pressure rubber hose clamp installation showing 3 mm (0.12 in) distance from hose end Instrument cluster display showing FUEL PRESS RELEASE mode - fuel pump will stop by touching START during IDLE; crank a few times after engine stall Fuse box diagrams for left-hand drive and right-hand drive vehicles showing fuel pump fuse location Photo showing fuel filter location in engine compartment

157.title: ENGINE MAINTENANCE
157.body:
ENGINE MAINTENANCE MA-12 The viscous paper type filter does not need cleaning between renewals. WARNING: Be careful not to burn yourself, as the engine oil is hot. Changing Engine Oil Warm up engine, and check for oil leakage from engine components. Remove drain plug and oil filler cap. Drain oil and refill with new engine oil. Check oil level. Start engine and check area around drain plug and oil filter for oil leakage. Run engine for a few minutes, then turn it off. After several minutes, check oil level. Refill oil capacity (Approximate) Condition Capacity (ℓ (Imp pt)) With oil filter change 3.1 (5-1/2) Without oil filter change 2.9 (5) The refill capacity changes depending on the oil temperature and drain time, use these values as a reference and be certain to check with the dipstick when changing the oil. CAUTION:
• Be sure to clean drain plug and install with new washer.
  Drain plug: 29 - 39 N·m (3.0 - 4.0 kg-m, 22 - 29 ft-lb)
• Use recommended engine oil (API SG grade). Drain plug torque 29 - 39 N·m Clean drain plug with new washer Drain plug torque 3.0 - 4.0 kg-m Clean drain plug with new washer Drain plug torque 22 - 29 ft-lb Clean drain plug with new washer Refill oil capacity (with oil filter change) 3.1 ℓ (5-1/2 Imp pt) With oil filter change Refill oil capacity (without oil filter change) 2.9 ℓ (5 Imp pt) Without oil filter change Air cleaner housing with clips/fasteners highlighted, showing how to open the air cleaner box Air cleaner filter element (viscous paper type) Engine showing oil filler cap, oil filter, and drain plug locations Oil drain plug washer showing oil pan side orientation Dipstick showing refill oil level mark — 'Refill oil to this level. Do not overfill.'

158.title: ENGINE MAINTENANCE
158.body: ENGINE MAINTENANCE MA-13 Changing Oil Filter Remove oil filter with Tool. Be careful not to burn yourself, as the engine and the engine oil are hot. Before installing new oil filter, clean the oil filter mounting surface on cylinder block, and coat the rubber seal of oil filter with a little engine oil. Screw in the oil filter until a slight resistance is felt, then tighten for a further 2/3 of a turn. Add engine oil. Refer to Changing Engine Oil. Checking and Changing Spark Plugs Disconnect ignition leads from spark plugs at boot. Do not pull on the lead. Remove spark plugs with spark plug wrench. Clean plugs in sand blast cleaner. Check insulator for cracks or chips, gasket for damage or deterioration and electrode for wear and burning. If excessively worn, replace with new spark plugs. Spark plug wrench size 16 mm (0.63 in) Wrench with a magnet to hold spark plug Using tool KV10115800 to remove oil filter Coating rubber seal of new oil filter with engine oil before installation Diagram showing 2/3 of a turn tightening for oil filter installation Illustration showing correct (O.K.) and incorrect (N.G.) methods for disconnecting ignition leads from spark plugs - correct at boot, not by pulling lead Spark plug wrench with magnet to hold spark plug, 16 mm (0.63 in)

Valve MA-14 Spark Plug Specifications Make CHAMPION NGK Standard type RC9YCC4 BKR5E-11 Hot type RC12YCC4 BKR4E-11 Cold type RC7YCC4 BKR6E-11 Checking and Changing Spark Plugs (Cont'd) Check spark plug gap. When removing P.C.V. valve from rocker cover, be sure to renew the seal. Lubricate new seal and P.C.V. valve before fitting. 160.title: ENGINE MAINTENANCE — Positive Crankcase Ventilation (P.C.V.) Valve (Cont'd) / Changing P.C.V. Filter / Checking Vacuum Hoses and Connections / Checking Vapor Lines
160.body: ENGINE MAINTENANCE — Positive Crankcase Ventilation (P.C.V.) Valve (Cont'd) / Changing P.C.V. Filter / Checking Vacuum Hoses and Connections / Checking Vapor Lines MA-15 Checking Ventilation Hoses Check hoses and hose connections for leaks. Disconnect all hoses and clean with compressed air. If any hose cannot be freed of obstructions, replace. Remove air cleaner cover and air cleaner filter, and replace P.C.V. filter. Check vacuum hoses for improper attachment and for leaks, cracks, damage, loose connections, chafing and deterioration. Refer to Vacuum Hose Drawing in ENGINE AND EMISSION CONTROL OVERALL SYSTEM in EF & EC section. Checking Vapor Lines Visually inspect vapor lines for improper attachment and for cracks, damage, loose connections, chafing and deterioration. Inspect vacuum relief valve of fuel tank filler cap for clogging, sticking, etc. Refer to EVAPORATIVE EMISSION CONTROL SYSTEM INSPECTION in EF & EC section. Photograph showing ventilation hoses in the engine bay Photograph showing P.C.V. filter location inside air cleaner housing Photograph showing purge line and vapor line routing in engine bay

161.title: Checking Exhaust Gas Sensor
161.body: Checking Exhaust Gas Sensor MA-16 Checking Exhaust Gas Sensor Procedure INSPECTION START Start engine and warm it up until water temperature indicator points to the middle of gauge. Method 1 (CONSULT): 1. See "M/R F/C MNT" in "Data monitor" mode. 2. Run engine at about 2,000 rpm for about 2 minutes under no-load. 3. Maintaining engine at 2,000 rpm under no-load (engine is warmed up sufficiently.), check that the monitor fluctuates between "LEAN" and "RICH" more than 5 times during 10 seconds. RICH → LEAN → RICH → 1 time  2 times LEAN → RICH ...... Method 2 (without CONSULT): 1. Set "Exhaust gas sensor monitor" in the self-diagnostic Mode II. (Refer to section EF & EC.) 2. Run engine at about 2,000 rpm for about 2 minutes under no-load. 3. Maintaining engine at 2,000 rpm under no-load, check to make sure that the RED L.E.D. on the E.C.U. or the check engine light on the instrument panel goes ON and OFF more than 5 times during 10 seconds. O.K. / N.G. INSPECTION END Check and adjustment should be made by referring to IDLE SPEED/IGNITION TIMING/IDLE MIXTURE RATIO INSPECTION in section EF & EC. OR O.K. N.G. Temperature gauge showing pointer at middle position between 120 and 270 CONSULT data monitor display showing M/R F/C MNT mode Diagnostic connector for CONSULT with IGN CHK terminal indicated ECU unit showing RED L.E.D. location

162.title: Chassis and Body Maintenance
162.body: Chassis and Body Maintenance MA-17 Check exhaust pipes, muffler and mounting for improper attachment and for leaks, cracks, damage, loose connections, chafing and deterioration. Check cable and links for improper attachment, chafing, wear and deterioration. Checking M/T Oil Check for oil leaks. Check oil level. Never start engine while checking oil level. Make sure the car is parked level. Check the oil level through speedometer cable hole. Use a straight wire or small ruler to measure the oil level from the top of the hole. The level should be in between "H" and "L". If topping up is required, add oil until "H". Never start engine while checking oil level. Make sure the car is parked level. Check the oil level through speedometer cable hole. Use a straight wire or small ruler to measure the oil level from the top of the hole. The level should be in between "H" and "L". If topping up is required, add oil until "H". M/T Oil Level L-H Range — Unit: mm (in) RS5F41A RS5F30A RS5F31V L - H 28 - 38 (1.10 - 1.50) 45 - 54 (1.77 - 2.13) 46 - 54 (1.81 - 2.13) Use recommended gear oil (API GL-4 grade) Oil capacity RS5F41A 3.1 - 3.3 ℓ (5-1/2 - 5-7/8 Imp pt) RS5F41A Oil capacity RS5F30A 2.8 - 3.0 ℓ (4-7/8 - 5-1/4 Imp pt) RS5F30A Oil capacity RS5F31V 2.8 - 3.0 ℓ (4-7/8 - 5-1/4 Imp pt) RS5F31V Drain plug torque RS55F41A 10 - 20 N·m (1.0-2.0 kg-m, 7-14 ft-lb) RS55F41A drain plug Drain plug torque RS55F30A 25 - 34 N·m (2.5-3.5 kg-m, 18-25 ft-lb) RS55F30A drain plug Drain plug torque RS55F31V 25 - 34 N·m (2.5-3.5 kg-m, 18-25 ft-lb) RS55F31V drain plug Three M/T variants referenced: RS5F41A, RS5F30A, RS5F31V. Oil level measurement via speedometer cable hole. Drain plug torque differs between RS55F41A (10-20 N·m) and RS55F30A/RS55F31V (25-34 N·m). Exhaust system showing pipes, muffler and mounting Clutch system showing cable and links Manual transmission showing oil leak check area with drop visible Diagram showing speedometer drive hole, straight wire, HIGH and LOW marks (H and L) for M/T oil level measurement Manual transmission showing drain plug location

163.title: Chassis and Body Maintenance — Checking and Changing N-CVT Fluid
163.body: Chassis and Body Maintenance — Checking and Changing N-CVT Fluid MA-18 Checking N-CVT Fluid Level Check for fluid leakage. Check fluid level. Fluid level should be checked using "HOT" range on dipstick at fluid temperatures of 50 to 80°C (122 to 176°F) after vehicle has been driven approximately 10 minutes in urban areas after engine is warmed up. But it can be checked at fluid temperatures of 30 to 50°C (86 to 122°F) using "COLD" range on dipstick for reference after engine is warmed up and before driving. However, fluid level must be rechecked using "HOT" range. Park vehicle on level surface and set parking brake. Start engine and then move selector lever through each gear range, ending in "P". Check fluid level with engine idling. Remove dipstick and wipe it clean with lint-free paper. Re-insert dipstick into charging pipe as far as it will go. Remove dipstick and note reading. If level is at low side of either range, add fluid through the speedometer cable hole. Check fluid condition. Check fluid for contamination. If fluid is very dark or smells burned, or contains frictional material check operation of N-CVT. Refer to section AT for checking operation of N-CVT. CAUTION: Do not overfill. Oil capacity (including cooler) 4.15 ℓ (7-5/16 Imp pt) including cooler Oil capacity (without cooler) 3.2 ℓ (5-5/8 Imp pt) without cooler Drain plug torque 23 - 27 N·m (2.34 - 2.75 kg-m, 17 - 20 ft-lb) Recommended fluid DEXRON IIE™ type A/T fluid N-CVT (Continuously Variable Transmission) equipped models only. Fluid level check procedure differs between COLD (30–50°C / 86–122°F) and HOT (50–80°C / 122–176°F) ranges on dipstick. Underside of vehicle showing fluid leakage check points (drip indicators) N-CVT dipstick showing COLD and HOT MIN/MAX ranges Technician checking fluid for contamination by examining dipstick N-CVT drain plug location on transmission pan (arrow indicating drain plug)

164.title: Chassis and Body Maintenance
164.body: Chassis and Body Maintenance MA-19 If fluid level is extremely low, check brake system for leaks, and brake pads for wear. Check brake fluid lines and parking brake cables for improper attachment, leaks, chafing, abrasion, deterioration, etc. Changing Brake Fluid Drain brake fluid from each air bleeder valve. Refill until new brake fluid comes out from each air bleeder valve. Use same procedure as in bleeding hydraulic system to refill brake fluid. Refer to section BR. Refill with recommended brake fluid "DOT 4". Never reuse drained brake fluid. Be careful not to splash brake fluid on painted areas. Check vacuum lines, connections and check valve for improper attachment, air tightness, chafing and deterioration. Brake fluid reservoir showing MAX and MIN level markings Brake fluid being drained/refilled at air bleeder valve with fluid container Engine bay view showing brake booster, vacuum hoses, connections and check valve with arrow indicating location

165.title: Checking Disc Brake
165.body: Checking Disc Brake MA-20 Check condition of disc brake components. Check condition and thickness. CL18SG Standard thickness 12 mm (0.472 in) CL18SG CL18SG Minimum thickness 10 mm (0.394 in) CL18SG CL7HG Standard thickness 7 mm (0.28 in) CL7HG CL7HG Minimum thickness 6 mm (0.24 in) CL7HG Check operation and for leakage. Check wear or damage. CL18SG Standard thickness 9.5 mm (0.37 in) CL18SG CL18SG Minimum thickness 2.0 mm (0.079 in) CL18SG CL7HG Standard thickness 9.5 mm (0.37 in) CL7HG CL7HG Minimum thickness 2.0 mm (0.079 in) CL7HG Caliper types CL18SG and CL7HG apply to different variants of the K11 Micra. Rotor and pad specs differ between caliper types. Illustration of disc brake rotor being checked Illustration of brake caliper assembly Illustration of brake pad being checked with measuring tool

166.title: Chassis and Body Maintenance
166.body: Chassis and Body Maintenance MA-21 Check condition of drum brake components. Check operation and for leakage. Check condition of inner surface. Standard inner diameter 180 mm (7.09 in) mm Maximum diameter 181.5 mm (7.146 in) mm Check for wear or damage. Standard thickness — Trailing 3.0 mm (0.118 in) mm Trailing shoe Standard thickness — Leading 4.5 mm (0.177 in) mm Leading shoe Minimum thickness 1.5 mm (0.059 in) mm Refer to section BR for shoe replacement. Remove inspection hole plug and check for lining wear. Balancing Wheels Adjust wheel balance using road wheel center. Refer to S.D.S. Drum brake assembly showing wheel cylinder and brake components Checking condition of inner surface of drum with inner diameter measurement Checking brake lining thickness with measuring tool Temporary method for checking lining wear via inspection hole plug removal

167.title: Chassis and Body Maintenance
167.body: Chassis and Body Maintenance MA-22 Wheel nuts 98 - 117 N·m Wheel nuts 10 - 12 kg-m Wheel nuts 72 - 87 ft-lb Steering Gear Inspection Check gear housing and boots for looseness, damage or grease leakage. Check connection with steering column for looseness. Steering Linkage Inspection Check ball joint, dust cover and other component parts for looseness, wear, damage or grease leakage. Check fluid level Fluid level should be checked using "HOT" range on dipstick at fluid temperatures of 50 to 80°C (122 to 176°F) or using "COLD" range on dipstick at fluid temperature of 0 to 30°C (32 to 86°F). Do not overfill. Recommended fluid is Automatic Transmission Fluid "DEXRON™" type. Power Steering Lines Inspection Check lines for improper attachment, leaks, cracks, damage, loose connections, chafing and deterioration. Tire rotation diagram showing radial tire rotation pattern: right front, right rear, left front, left rear, and spare tire positions with arrows indicating rotation direction Steering gear and linkage assembly showing check grease leakage and check tightening torque callouts Power steering fluid reservoir showing dipstick/fluid level check Power steering lines and connections on the vehicle

168.title: Chassis and Body Maintenance
168.body: Chassis and Body Maintenance MA-23 Vehicle diagram indicating lubrication points for hood latches, locks, and hinges Hood latch (front) Hood latch/lock (rear/engine bay) Door hinge (left side) Hood/body hinge or latch detail Fuel filler or door lock detail Checking Seat Belts, Buckles, Retractors, Anchors and Adjusters If the vehicle has been involved in a collision or has been overturned, replace all seat belt assemblies, regardless of nature of accident. If the condition of any component of a seat belt is questionable, do not have seat belt repaired, but replaced as a belt assembly. If webbing is cut, frayed, or damaged, replace belt assembly. Do not spill drinks, oil, etc. on inner lap belt buckle. Never oil tongue and buckle. Use a NISSAN genuine seat belt assembly. Anchor bolt 43 - 55 N·m Seat belt anchor bolt torque Anchor bolt 4.4 - 5.6 kg-m Seat belt anchor bolt torque Anchor bolt 32 - 41 ft-lb Seat belt anchor bolt torque Adjuster torque 43 - 55 N·m Seat belt adjuster Adjuster torque 4.4 - 5.6 kg-m Seat belt adjuster Adjuster torque 32 - 41 ft-lb Seat belt adjuster Check anchors torque 43 - 55 N·m Check anchors for loose mounting Check anchors torque 4.4 - 5.6 kg-m Check anchors for loose mounting Check anchors torque 32 - 41 ft-lb Check anchors for loose mounting Seat belt inspection diagram showing key check points and torque values Adjuster — 43-55 N·m (4.4-5.6 kg-m, 32-41 ft-lb) Check anchors for loose mounting — 43-55 N·m (4.4-5.6 kg-m, 32-41 ft-lb) Cover must be fitted Check belts for damage Anchor bolt — 43-55 N·m (4.4-5.6 kg-m, 32-41 ft-lb) Check retractor for smooth operation Check functioning of buckles and tongues After a frontal collision – with or without occupants – the rear cushion striker assemblies must be replaced. (Spil cushion only) N·m (kg-m, ft-lb) Vehicle diagram showing lubrication points for hood latches, locks and hinges with inset detail photos of each location Diagram showing seat belt checking points including adjuster, anchors, retractor, buckles/tongues, and belts for damage, with torque values. Also shows rear seat cushion striker assembly note.

169.title: Checking Body Corrosion
169.body: Checking Body Corrosion MA-24 Visually check the body sheet metal panel for corrosion, paint damage (scratches, chipping, rubbing, etc.) or damage to the anti-corrosion materials. In particular, check the following locations. Hood front end, door lower end, trunk lid rear end, etc. Side sill of rear fender and center pillar, rear wheel housing of rear fender, around strut tower in engine compartment, etc. Trunk lid opening, sun roof opening, fender wheel-arch flange, fuel filler lid flange, around holes in panel, etc. Waist moulding, windshield moulding, bumper, etc. Damage or condition of mudguard, fender protector, chipping protector, etc. Damage or separation of anti-corrosion materials under the body. Condition of drain holes at door and side sill. When repairing corroded areas, refer to the Corrosion Repair Manual.

175.title: PERIODIC MAINTENANCE (For Europe) — MAINTENANCE UNDER SEVERE DRIVING CONDITIONS
175.body:
PERIODIC MAINTENANCE (For Europe) — MAINTENANCE UNDER SEVERE DRIVING CONDITIONS MA-7004 The maintenance intervals shown on the preceding pages are for normal operating conditions. If the vehicle is mainly operated under severe driving conditions as shown below, more frequent maintenance must be performed on the following items as shown in the table. A — Driving under dusty conditions
B — Driving repeatedly short distances
C — Towing a trailer or caravan
D — Extensive idling
E — Driving in extremely adverse weather conditions or in areas where ambient temperatures are either extremely low or extremely high
F — Driving in high humidity areas or in mountainous areas
G — Driving in areas using salt or other corrosive materials
H — Driving on rough and/or muddy roads or in the desert
I — Driving with frequent use of braking or in mountainous areas
J — Frequent off road use or driving in water
K — Sustained high speed driving
L — Low quality lubricants or fuel Severe Driving Conditions Maintenance Schedule Driving condition Maintenance item Engine Maintenance operation Maintenance interval A B C D Engine oil & engine oil filter CG10DE/CGA3DE Replace Every 7,500 km (4,500 miles) or 6 months A Air cleaner filter (viscous paper type) CG10DE/CGA3DE Replace Every 30,000 km (18,000 miles) or 24 months A . . . E Fuel filter CG10DE/CGA3DE Replace Every 45,000 km (27,000 miles) or 36 months A PCV filter CG10DE/CGA3DE Replace Every 15,000 km (9,000 miles) or 12 months L Front heated oxygen sensor CG10DE/CGA3DE Inspect Every 30,000 km (18,000 miles) or 24 months L Rear heated oxygen sensor CG10DE/CGA3DE Inspect Every 30,000 km (18,000 miles) or 24 months A Ventilation air filter CG10DE/CGA3DE Replace Every 15,000 km (9,000 miles) or 12 months F Brake fluid CG10DE/CGA3DE Replace Every 15,000 km (9,000 miles) or 12 months C . . . H Standard differential gear oil CG10DE/CGA3DE Replace Every 30,000 km (18,000 miles) or 24 months C . . . H CVT fluid CG10DE/CGA3DE Replace Every 15,000 km (9,000 miles) or 12 months A . C . . . G H I Brake pads/linings, discs/drums & other brake system components CG10DE/CGA3DE Inspect Every 7,500 km (4,500 miles) or 6 months G H Steering gear & linkage, axle & suspension parts, drive shafts & exhaust system CG10DE/CGA3DE Inspect Every 15,000 km (9,000 miles) or 12 months Applicable engines: CG10DE and CGA3DE. For Europe only. Annual driving distance less than 30,000 km/year.

182.title: Periodic Maintenance (Except for Europe) — Maintenance Under Severe Driving Conditions
182.body:
Periodic Maintenance (Except for Europe) — Maintenance Under Severe Driving Conditions MA-7011 The maintenance intervals shown on the preceding pages are for normal operating conditions. If the vehicle is mainly operated under severe driving conditions as shown below, more frequent maintenance must be performed on the following items as shown in the table. A — Driving under dusty conditions
B — Driving repeatedly short distances
C — Towing a trailer or caravan
D — Extensive idling
E — Driving in extremely adverse weather conditions or in areas where ambient temperatures are either extremely low or extremely high
F — Driving in high humidity areas or in mountainous areas
G — Driving in areas using salt or other corrosive materials
H — Driving on rough and/or muddy roads or in the desert
I — Driving with frequent use of braking or in mountainous areas Severe Driving Conditions Maintenance Schedule Driving condition Maintenance item Maintenance operation Maintenance interval A Air cleaner filter   A PCV filter Replace More frequently A B C D Engine oil (petrol engine) Replace Every 5,000 km (3,000 miles) or 3 months A B C D Engine oil (diesel engine) Replace More frequently A B C D Engine oil filter Replace Every 5,000 km (3,000 miles) or 3 months A E Fuel filter Replace Every 20,000 km (12,000 miles) F Brake fluid Replace or 12 months C H CVT fluid Replace Every 20,000 km (12,000 miles) or 12 months C H Differential gear oil Replace Every 40,000 km (24,000 miles) or 12 months G H Steering gear, linkage & transfer gear, axle & suspension parts, propeller shafts & front drive shafts Check Every 10,000 km (6,000 miles) or 6 months for passenger vehicles A C G H I Brake pads, rotors & other brake components Check Every 5,000 km (3,000 miles) or 3 months A C G H I Brake linings, drums & other brake components Check Every 10,000 km (6,000 miles) or 6 months G Lock, hinges & bonnet latch Lubricate Every 5,000 km (3,000 miles) or 3 months A Ventilation air filter Replace More frequently Maintenance operation: Check = Check and correct or replace if necessary. Whenever you drive off-road through sand, mud or water, more frequent maintenance may be required of the following items:
• Brake pads and rotors
• Brake lining and drums
• Brake lines and hoses
• Wheel bearing grease
• Differential, transfer gear oil and automatic transmission fluid
• Differential gear oil and CVT fluid
• Steering linkage
• Propeller shaft and front drive shaft
• Air cleaner filter
• Clutch housing (check water entry) Except for Europe. Covers petrol and diesel engine variants, CVT transmission.

184.title: Checking CVT Fluid Level System
184.body: Checking CVT Fluid Level System MA-7013 Checking CVT Fluid Level System Check for fluid leakage. Check fluid level. Fluid level should be checked using "HOT" range on dipstick at fluid temperatures of 50 to 80°C (122 to 176°F) after vehicle has been driven approximately 10 minutes in urban areas after engine is warmed up. But it can be checked at fluid temperatures of 30 to 50°C (86 to 122°F) using "COLD" range on dipstick for reference after engine is warmed up and before driving. However, fluid level must be rechecked using "HOT" range. Park vehicle on level surface and set parking brake. Start engine and then move selector lever through each gear range, ending in "P". Check fluid level with engine idling. Remove dipstick and wipe it clean with lint-free paper. Re-insert dipstick into charging pipe as far as it will go. Remove dipstick and note reading. If level is at low side of either range, add fluid through the speedometer cable hole. Do not overfill. Firmly fix the dipstick using a lip attached to the fluid charging pipe. Check fluid condition Check fluid condition. Check fluid for contamination. If fluid is very dark, smells burned or contains frictional material check operation of CVT. Refer to section AT for checking operation of CVT. CVT (Continuously Variable Transmission) equipped vehicles only. Dipstick has both COLD and HOT MIN/MAX ranges labeled H-CVT. Underside of vehicle showing fluid leakage drip points CVT dipstick showing COLD (MIN/MAX) and HOT (MIN/MAX) ranges with H-CVT label A/T fluid level gauge and A/T fluid charging pipe with stopper positions (when fixing and when releasing) labeled Technician checking fluid for contamination by wiping dipstick on lint-free paper

185.title: Chassis and Body Maintenance
185.body: Chassis and Body Maintenance MA-7014 Changing CVT Fluid Warm up CVT fluid by driving the vehicle for 10 minutes. Drain CVT fluid from radiator cooler hose (return side) and refill with new CVT fluid at charging pipe when engine running at idle speed. Refill until new CVT fluid comes out from radiator cooler hose (return side). About 30 to 50% extra fluid will be required for this procedure. Fluid capacity — Hyper CVT Approx. 5.0 l Use genuine NISSAN CVT fluid (NS-1) or exact equivalent. Check condition of disc brake components. Check condition and thickness. CL18VG Rotor Standard thickness 18 mm (0.789 in) CL18VG Rotor Minimum thickness 16 mm (0.630 in) Check wear or damage. CL18VG Pad Standard thickness 9.5 mm (0.37 in) CL18VG Pad Minimum thickness 2.0 mm (0.079 in) CVT fluid procedure applies to Hyper CVT models. Disc brake specs given for CL18VG caliper/rotor specification. CVT fluid drain/fill location on transmission casing Checking disc brake rotor with measurement tool Checking disc brake pad thickness with caliper

195.title: PRECAUTIONS
195.body:
PRECAUTIONS EM-2 Some important engine parts are tightened using an angular-tightening method rather than a torque setting method. If these parts are tightened using a torque setting method, dispersal of the tightening force (axial bolt force) will be two or three times that of the dispersal produced by using the correct angular-tightening method. To assure the satisfactory maintenance of the engine, bolts and nuts must be tightened using an angular tightening method. Before tightening the bolts and nuts, ensure that the thread and seating surfaces are clean and then coated with engine oil. The bolts and nuts which require the angular-tightening method are as follows:
(1) Cylinder head bolts
(2) Main bearing cap bolts
(3) Connecting rod cap nuts. Tightening step 1 38 N·m Typical tightening sequence Tightening step 2 70 N·m Typical tightening sequence Tightening step 3 0 N·m Typical tightening sequence Tightening step 4 40 N·m Typical tightening sequence Tightening step 5 (angular) 60 ° Typical tightening sequence Liquid Gasket Application Procedure Before applying liquid gasket, use a scraper to remove all traces of old liquid gasket from mating surface. Apply a continuous bead of liquid gasket to mating surfaces. (Use Genuine Liquid Gasket or equivalent.)
• Be sure liquid gasket is 3.5 to 4.5 mm (0.138 to 0.177 in) wide (for oil pan).
• Be sure liquid gasket is 2.0 to 3.0 mm (0.079 to 0.118 in) wide (in areas except oil pan). Apply liquid gasket to inner surface around hole perimeter area. (Assembly should be done within 5 minutes after coating.) Wait at least 30 minutes before refilling engine oil and engine coolant. Liquid gasket bead width (oil pan) 3.5 to 4.5 mm (0.138 to 0.177 in) Oil pan Liquid gasket bead width (areas except oil pan) 2.0 to 3.0 mm (0.079 to 0.118 in) Areas except oil pan Assembly time after coating within 5 minutes After applying liquid gasket Wait time before refilling fluids at least 30 minutes Before refilling engine oil and coolant Typical tightening sequence showing four bolts at stages: 38 N·m, 70 N·m, 0 N·m, 40 N·m, then 60° Diagram showing liquid gasket application: groove and bolt hole on mating surface, with arrow indicating inner side application

196.title: PRECAUTIONS
196.body:
PRECAUTIONS EM-2 Some important engine parts are tightened using an angular-tightening method rather than a torque setting method. If these parts are tightened using a torque setting method, dispersal of the tightening force (axial bolt force) will be two or three times that of the dispersal produced by using the correct angular-tightening method. To assure the satisfactory maintenance of the engine, bolts and nuts must be tightened using an angular tightening method. Before tightening the bolts and nuts, ensure that the thread and seating surfaces are clean and then coated with engine oil. The bolts and nuts which require the angular-tightening method are as follows:
(1) Cylinder head bolts
(2) Main bearing cap bolts
(3) Connecting rod cap nuts. Tightening sequence step 1 38 N·m Initial torque Tightening sequence step 2 70 N·m Second torque Tightening sequence step 3 0 N·m Loosen completely Tightening sequence step 4 40 N·m Re-torque Tightening sequence step 5 60 ° Final angular tighten Liquid Gasket Application Procedure Before applying liquid gasket, use a scraper to remove all traces of old liquid gasket from mating surface. Apply a continuous bead of liquid gasket to mating surfaces. (Use Genuine Liquid Gasket or equivalent.)
• Be sure liquid gasket is 4.0 to 5.0 mm (0.157 to 0.197 in) wide (for oil pan).
• Be sure liquid gasket is 2.0 to 3.0 mm (0.079 to 0.118 in) wide (in areas except oil pan). Apply liquid gasket to inner surface around hole perimeter area. (Assembly should be done within 5 minutes after coating.) Wait at least 30 minutes before refilling engine oil and engine coolant. Typical tightening sequence diagram showing 5 stages: 38 N·m, 70 N·m, 0 N·m, 40 N·m, 60° Liquid gasket application diagram showing groove, bolt hole, and inner side with arrow indicating direction
