443.title: CYLINDER HEAD — Removal
443.body:
CYLINDER HEAD — Removal EM-7015 CAUTION:
• After removing timing chain, do not turn crankshaft and camshaft separately, or valves will strike piston heads.
• When installing chain tensioner, oil seats, or other sliding parts, lubricate contacting surfaces with new engine oil.
• Apply new engine oil to bolt threads and seat surface when installing camshaft sprocket and crankshaft pulley.
• When removing oil pump assembly, remove camshaft position sensore (PHASE), then remove timing chain from engine.
• Be careful not to damage sensor edges. Cylinder Head Removal Drain engine coolant from radiator and cylinder block. Be careful not to spill coolant on drive belts. Remove the alternator and power steering drive belts. Remove air cleaner. Remove front right-side wheel. Remove front and right-side splashguards. Remove front exhaust tube. Remove ignition coils. Remove rocker cover. Loosen bolts in numerical order as shown in figure Remove all spark plugs. Set No. 1 piston at T.D.C. on compression stroke. Engine bay view showing alternator and power steering drive belts with arrow indicating belt location Top view of engine showing ignition coils with numbered loosening sequence for rocker cover bolts (loosen in numerical order 1-10) Crankshaft pulley showing timing marks: TDC, 5°, 10°, 15°, 20° with pointer indicator

444.title: CYLINDER HEAD — Removal (Cont'd)
444.body: CYLINDER HEAD — Removal (Cont'd) EM-7016 Cylinder Head Removal (Continued) Remove camshaft position sensor (PHASE). Do not allow any magnetic materials to contzact the camshaft position sensor (PHASE). Be careful not to damage sensor. Remove cam sprocket cover. Remove thermostat housing. Remove chain tensioner. Loosen idler sprocket bolt. Remove camshaft sprocket bolts. Remove camshaft sprockets. Remove camshaft brackets and camshafsts. These parts should be reassembled in their original positions. Bolts should be loosened in two or three steps. Remove idler sprocket bolt. Remove cylinder head with manifolds. Head warpage or cracking could result from removing bolts in incorrect order. Cylinder head bolts should be loosened in two or three steps. Remove upper timing chain. Loosen in numerical order. Loosen in numerical order. Camshaft position sensor (PHASE) removal location on engine Thermostat housing removal with arrows indicating bolts Chain tensioner, idler sprocket bolt, camshaft sprocket bolts and sprockets removal Camshaft brackets loosen order diagram — numbered loosen sequence Cylinder head bolt loosen order diagram — numbered loosen sequence

445.title: CYLINDER HEAD — Installation
445.body: CYLINDER HEAD — Installation EM-7017 Installation Confirm that No. 1 piston is set at T.D.C. on its compression stroke. Set idler sprocket by aligning the mating mark on the larger sprocket with the yellow mating mark on the lower timing chain. Install upper timing chain and set it by aligning the mating mark on the smaller sprocket with the silver mating marks on the upper timing chain. Make sure sprocket's mating mark faces engine front. Install idler sprocket shaft from the rear side. Ensure that the shaft is located correctly, as shown. Install cylinder head with new gasket. Do not rotate crankshaft and camshaft separately, or valves will strike piston heads. Before installing cylinder head gasket, apply a continuous bead of liquid gasket as shown in the figure. Be sure to install washers between bolts and cylinder head. Make sure sprocket's mating mark faces engine front. Ensure that the shaft is located correctly, as shown. Before installing cylinder head gasket, apply a continuous bead of liquid gasket as shown in the figure. Be sure to install washers between bolts and cylinder head. Do not rotate crankshaft and camshaft separately, or valves will strike piston heads. Liquid gasket bead width on front cover mating surface 2.0 - 3.0 mm (0.079 - 0.118 in) Before installing cylinder head gasket Crankshaft pulley with timing pointer showing TDC, 5°, 10°, 15°, 20° marks Timing chain alignment diagram showing upper and lower timing chains, mating marks (silver, bronze, yellow), idler sprocket with link counts (5 links, 7 links, 11 links) Idler shaft installation showing dowel and idler shaft location Front cover liquid gasket application area showing 2.0–3.0 mm (0.079–0.118 in) bead location on cylinder block mating surface

446.title: CYLINDER HEAD — Installation (Cont'd)
446.body: CYLINDER HEAD — Installation (Cont'd) EM-7018 Tightening procedure (bolts 1-10) Tighten bolts to 39 N·m (4.0 kg-m, 29 ft-lb). [Step a] Tighten bolts to 78 N·m (8.0 kg-m, 58 ft-lb). [Step b] Loosen bolts completely. [Step c] Tighten bolts to 35 N·m (3.6 kg-m, 26 ft-lb). [Step d] Turn bolts 60 degrees clockwise. [Step e] Tighten bolts (11 - 15) to 6.3 to 8.3 N·m (0.64 to 0.85 kg-m, 4.6 to 6.1 ft-lb). [Step f] Install idler sprocket bolt. Tightening torque N·m (kg-m, ft-lb) Bolt Group a b c d e Bolts (1 - 10) 39 (4.0, 29) 78 (8.0, 58) 0 (0,0) 35 (3.6, 26) 60 degrees Bolts (11 - 15) — — — — 6.3 - 8.3 (0.64 - 0.85, 4.6 - 6.1) Cylinder head bolt washer orientation — washer shown with chamfered side toward cylinder head side Cylinder head bolt tightening sequence diagram — bolts numbered 1-15 tighten in numerical order Graph showing tightening torque procedure stages a through e for cylinder head bolts — Y-axis: Tightening torque N·m (kg-m, ft-lb); stages a=39(4.0,29), b=78(8.0,58), c=loosen completely, d=35(3.6,26), e=60 degrees

447.title: CYLINDER HEAD — Installation (Cont'd)
447.body: CYLINDER HEAD — Installation (Cont'd) EM-7019 Installation (Cont'd) Install camshaft and camshaft brackets. Tighten camshaft bracket bolts gradually in three stages: (a) 2 N·m (0.2 kg-m, 1.4 ft-lb) (b) 6 N·m (0.6 kg-m, 4.3 ft-lb) (c) 9 - 11.8 N·m (0.92 - 1.20 kg-m, 6.7 - 8.7 ft-lb) If any part of valve assembly or camshaft is replaced, check valve clearance according to the reference data. After completing assembly, check valve clearance. Refer to 'Checking' and 'Adjusting' in 'VALVE CLEARANCE'. Reference data for initial setting of valve clearance (Cold): Intake 0.30 mm (0.012 in), Exhaust 0.35 mm (0.014 in) Assemble camshaft sprocket with chain. Set timing chain by aligning mating marks with those of camshaft sprockets. Make sure sprocket's mating marks face engine front. Install camshaft sprocket bolts. Make sure camshafts are aligned as shown in figure. Camshaft bracket bolt torque — Stage a 2 N·m Stage a Camshaft bracket bolt torque — Stage a 0.2 kg-m Stage a Camshaft bracket bolt torque — Stage a 1.4 ft-lb Stage a Camshaft bracket bolt torque — Stage b 6 N·m Stage b Camshaft bracket bolt torque — Stage b 0.6 kg-m Stage b Camshaft bracket bolt torque — Stage b 4.3 ft-lb Stage b Camshaft bracket bolt torque — Stage c 9 - 11.8 N·m Stage c Camshaft bracket bolt torque — Stage c 0.92 - 1.20 kg-m Stage c Camshaft bracket bolt torque — Stage c 6.7 - 8.7 ft-lb Stage c Valve clearance (Cold) — Intake 0.30 mm Cold, initial setting reference data Valve clearance (Cold) — Intake 0.012 in Cold, initial setting reference data Valve clearance (Cold) — Exhaust 0.35 mm Cold, initial setting reference data Valve clearance (Cold) — Exhaust 0.014 in Cold, initial setting reference data Timing chain mating mark positions 5 links 7 links 11 links Mating mark (silver) Mating mark (silver) Mating mark (bronze) Mating mark Camshaft knock pin alignment for sprocket installation Knock pin Knock pin Tensioner body Ratchet Spring Plunger Piston Camshaft bracket bolt tightening order shown in numerical sequence on cylinder head diagram Timing chain assembly showing mating marks (silver and bronze), link counts (5 links, 7 links, 11 links) and camshaft sprockets Camshaft sprocket installation showing knock pin alignment on both intake and exhaust camshafts Chain tensioner components: tensioner body, ratchet, spring, plunger, and piston

448.title: CYLINDER HEAD - Installation (Cont'd)
448.body:
CYLINDER HEAD - Installation (Cont'd) EM-7020 Installation (Cont'd) Assemble the tensioner by rotating and pushing the ratchet up the spiral until it stops. The tensioner is now pre-set. Chain lash will automatically set the tensioner when the engine is started. Install the tensioner. CAUTION: Check no problems occur when engine is rotated. CAUTION: Make sure that No. 1 piston is set at T.D.C. on its compression stroke. Apply liquid gasket to thermostat housing; Ensure mating surfaces are clean and free from dirt, grease and traces of old liquid gasket. Install thermostat housing. Apply liquid gasket to cam sprocket cover as illustrated. Then install cam sprocket cover. Clean the rocker cover, ensure it is free from grease. Fit the gasket & cover within 5 minutes of applying the liquid gasket, as follows: CAUTION:
• Check no problems occur when engine is rotated.
• Make sure that No. 1 piston is set at T.D.C. on its compression stroke. Ensure mating surfaces are clean and free from dirt, grease and traces of old liquid gasket. Fit the gasket & cover within 5 minutes of applying the liquid gasket, as follows: Liquid gasket bead width - thermostat housing 2.0 - 3.0 mm (0.079 - 0.118 in) Application to thermostat housing mating surface Liquid gasket bead width - cam sprocket cover 2.0 - 3.0 mm (0.079 - 0.118 in) Application to cam sprocket cover mating surface Illustration showing hands rotating and pushing the ratchet up the spiral on the tensioner piston assembly View of timing chain area showing tensioner installed with chain and sprockets visible Thermostat housing with liquid gasket application bead shown at 2.0-3.0 mm (0.079-0.118 in) Cam sprocket cover showing liquid gasket application points with bead dimension 2.0-3.0 mm (0.079-0.118 in)

449.title: CYLINDER HEAD - Installation (Cont'd)
449.body: CYLINDER HEAD - Installation (Cont'd) EM-7021 Liquid gasket bead width (plan view, two locations) 3.0 (0.118) mm (in) Applied to cylinder head cover before fitting gasket Installation (Cont'd) Apply liquid gasket to the cylinder head as shown. Fit the gasket to the cover. Fit the rocker cover to the cylinder head. Tighten the bolts in the correct order, as shown. Recheck bolts. Check a third time. Install camshaft position sensor (PHASE). Install all spark plugs and ignition coils. Install engine front mounting bracket. Install front exhaust tube. Install the front and side splashguards. Install front right wheel. Install air cleaner. Install and correctly tension the alternator and power steering drive belts. Refer to MA section for correct tension. Refer to MA section for correct tension. Plan view showing liquid gasket application points on cylinder head cover. Two sealant application dimensions of 3.0 (0.118) mm indicated. Shows sealant bead location relative to cylinder head. Top view of rocker cover showing bolt tightening sequence numbered 1 through 10. Caption states: Tighten in numerical order.

462.title: PRECAUTIONS — Service Notice
462.body: PRECAUTIONS — Service Notice AT-5 Before proceeding with disassembly, thoroughly clean the outside of the transaxle. It is important to prevent the internal parts from becoming contaminated by dirt or other foreign matter. Disassembly should be done in a clean work area. Use lint-free cloth or towels for wiping parts clean. Common shop rags can leave fibers that could interfere with the operation of the transaxle. When disassembling parts, place them in order in a parts rack so that they can be put back into the unit in their proper positions. All parts should be carefully cleaned with a general purpose, non-flammable solvent before inspection or reassembly. Gaskets, seals, O-rings, circlips and split pins should be replaced any time the transaxle is disassembled. It is very important to perform functional tests whenever they are indicated. The valve body contains precision parts and requires extreme care when removed during service. Before assembly, apply a coat of recommended A.T.F. to all parts. Petroleum jelly may be applied to O-rings and seals and used to hold small bearings and washers in place during reassembly. Do not use grease. Extreme care should be taken to avoid damage to O-rings, seals and gaskets when assembling. After overhaul, refill the transaxle with new A.T.F.

463.title: INTRODUCTION — RE0F05A NISSAN CONTINUOUSLY VARIABLE TRANSMISSION
463.body: INTRODUCTION — RE0F05A NISSAN CONTINUOUSLY VARIABLE TRANSMISSION AT-6 The Nissan Continuously Variable Transmission (N·CVT) is an electronically controlled steel belt/pulley drive system, giving variable ratios and stepless speed changes from starting to overdrive. Hydraulic pressure acts upon two split pulleys, varying the contact radius (or groove width) of each, causing a steel belt to alter input or output speed. The pressure applied to the pulleys is determined by engine speed, engine torque, accelerator pedal position, etc. Signals are processed by an Electronic Control Unit, which in turn controls the hydraulic system via a control valve assembly. An electromagnetic powder clutch provides drive between engine and transmission. Forward to reverse changeover is by conventional synchroniser assembly. An engine braking effect occurs during deceleration in the "Ds" mode. Cross-section of RE0F05A CVT with labelled components Electromagnetic powder clutch Primary pulley Forward and reverse changeover system Oil pump Steel belt Secondary pulley Final gear Reduction gear Applies to Nissan Micra K11 equipped with RE0F05A N·CVT (Nissan Continuously Variable Transmission). Cross-sectional diagram of the RE0F05A Nissan CVT showing internal components including electromagnetic powder clutch, primary pulley, forward and reverse changeover system, oil pump, steel belt, secondary pulley, final gear, reduction gear.

464.title: FUNCTION
464.body: FUNCTION AT-7 The N·CVT consists of four main systems: N·CVT Main Systems Electromagnetic Powder Clutch (including ECU control circuitry). Forward and reverse synchromesh changeover system. Steel belt and pulleys. Final drive reduction gears. The electromagnetic powder clutch operates on basic principles of physics – electricity and magnetism. When iron powder is magnetised, the particles become linked in chain fashion, and the powder, put simply, "solidifies". If a small gap between driving and driven members is filled with powder, and current applied to it, then the members become connected and transmission torque (drive) is generated. The transmission torque is proportional to the amount of current supplied. Current is supplied via coils located in the driven member. When the current flow is cut off, the transmission torque disappears. Electromagnetic Powder Clutch cross-section and operating principle diagrams Drive member Coil Driven member Metal powder Current Coil Magnetic field Drive member Powder Driven member engaged disengaged Applies to N·CVT (automatic transmission variant) of K11 Nissan Micra 1993-2003. Electromagnetic Powder Clutch cross-section diagram showing Drive member, Coil, Driven member, Metal powder, and Current input on left; schematic diagrams on right showing engaged (with Coil, Magnetic field, Drive member, Powder, Driven member) and disengaged states

465.title: FUNCTION — Clutch Control
465.body: FUNCTION — Clutch Control AT-8 Electrical current is supplied to the clutch via carbon brushes making contact with a slip ring attached to the clutch housing. The clutch is applied and released by controlling the current to the coils located in the driven member. The electronic control unit (ECU) receives input signals, processes them and controls the clutch accordingly. Clutch control also prevents "shock" (direct coupling/uncoupling) when engine braking is used in the "Ds" position. The control circuits are self-diagnostic. Faults can be detected using CONSULT or the On-board Diagnostic (blink code) System. N-CVT Control Unit receives the following input signals and outputs clutch current to the electromagnetic powder clutch. Input signals Engine revolutional signal Accelerator signal Brake signal Throttle position signal Selector position signals Vehicle speed Water temperature signal Air conditioner signal ABS signal N-CVT Control Unit Clutch current Drive member Coil Powder Driven member Applies to N-CVT (Nissan Continuously Variable Transmission) automatic transmission variant of K11 Micra. Diagram showing N-CVT Control Unit receiving input signals and outputting clutch current to the electromagnetic powder clutch assembly, with callouts for Drive member, Coil, Powder, and Driven member.

467.title: FUNCTION — N·CVT ELECTRONIC CONTROL UNIT
467.body: FUNCTION — N·CVT ELECTRONIC CONTROL UNIT AT-10 N·CVT Electronic Control Unit INHIBIT SW. IGN. SIGNAL STARTER MOTOR BRAKE LAMP REVERSE LAMP ACCELERATOR SW. THROTTLE POS. SW. VEHICLE SPEED SENSOR CONSULT STOPLAMP SW. N·CVT CONTROL UNIT N·CVT RELAY IDLE UP RELAY BATTERY FICD SOLENOID ABS CONTROL UNIT CHECK LAMP ELECTROMAGNETIC CLUTCH LINE PRESSURE SOLENOID ENGINE CONTROL UNIT POWER (ACC) AIRCON RELAY AIRCON COMPRESSOR IGN SW. INHIBIT SWITCH DETAILS Pin P R N D Ds Function 1 0 0 0 0 0 IGN SW 2 0     RANGE SW-P 3 0     RANGE SW-R 4   0   RANGE SW-N 5    0  RANGE SW-D 6     0 RANGE SW-Ds 7 0  0   IGN ST. SW 8 0  0   ST MTR When starting, current is supplied to the clutch proportional to the cranking speed. A micro switch on the accelerator pedal detects pedal movement and tells the ECU to energise the clutch. The switch also prevents stalling when the vehicle comes to a halt. Throttle movement is detected by the ECU, and clutch current is adjusted accordingly. Brake system operation is monitored, and clutch current modified accordingly. Switches located adjacent to the selector lever detect "D", "Ds" and "R" modes. A vehicle speed sensor is located in the transmission casing, and senses output shaft speed. Clutch current is adjusted to compensate for variations in engine temperature, ie if the engine is cold, the stalling point is raised. Aircon operation is sensed, and clutch current is adjusted to ensure that the engine stalling point is correct. Clutch current is switched off during ABS operation. N·CVT (Nissan Continuously Variable Transmission) automatic transmission variant. Inhibit switch details show P, R, N, D, Ds selector positions. N·CVT Electronic Control Unit wiring schematic showing all input and output connections including inhibit switch details table, starter motor, brake lamp, reverse lamp, accelerator SW, throttle pos. SW, vehicle speed sensor, CONSULT connector, stoplamp SW, N·CVT relay, idle up relay, battery, FICD solenoid, ABS control unit, check lamp, electromagnetic clutch, line pressure solenoid, engine control unit, aircon relay, aircon compressor, and power (ACC).

468.title: FUNCTION — OUTPUT SIGNALS
468.body:
FUNCTION — OUTPUT SIGNALS AT-11 There are five output modes to ensure smooth operation of the electromagnetic clutch: Electromagnetic Clutch Output Modes Chart (UAT014) Engine Revolution Selector Position Accelerator Vehicle Speed (km/h) Mode UNDER 200 RPM — — All ranges (4) REVERSE EXCITATION MODE — N,P RANGE — All ranges (4) REVERSE EXCITATION MODE OVER 200 RPM D RANGE RELEASED < 7/8 (3) DRAG MODE OVER 200 RPM D RANGE RELEASED 8/12 – 16/20 (5) ZERO MODE OVER 200 RPM D RANGE RELEASED > 16/20 (2) DIRECT-COUPLING MODE OVER 200 RPM Ds,R RANGE RELEASED < 7/8 (3) DRAG MODE OVER 200 RPM Ds,R RANGE RELEASED > 7/8 (2) DIRECT-COUPLING MODE OVER 200 RPM D RANGE PRESSED All ranges (1) STARTING MODE OVER 200 RPM Ds,R RANGE PRESSED All ranges (1) STARTING MODE Controls the clutch when pulling away by increasing current in proportion to engine speed and rate of increase of engine speed. Decel/accel shock when travelling at low speed (below 20 km/h) is eliminated by delaying clutch "engagement" until the N·CVT input shaft speed coincides (almost) with engine speed. Directly couples the clutch when the vehicle reaches a preset speed. Maintains good low speed (below 7 km/h) characteristics by passing a small current through the clutch (approx. 0.2A) to create a "drag torque" when the accelerator pedal is released. Also prevents "creeping" and "stalling" by continuously feeding idle speed signals back to the ECU. Demagnetises the clutch powder (to remove residual magnetism and prevent non-releasing of the clutch) when: a) The selector lever is in "N" or "P".
b) Engine speed is 200rev/min or less. This also prevents the vehicle from being push-started. De-energises the clutch while the output mode is shifting from mode 2 to mode 3 as the accelerator pedal is released in "D", "Ds" or "R". Controls line pressure solenoid to determine torque transmission. Controls idle-up relay to maintain oil pressure during ABS operation. Applies to N·CVT (automatic transmission variant) of K11 Nissan Micra. Selector positions referenced: D, Ds, R, N, P ranges. Table/chart showing electromagnetic clutch output modes based on engine revolution, selector position, accelerator state, and vehicle speed ranges (7/8, 8/12, 16/20, 18.5/22.5, 30/35 km/h). Maps five modes: (1) Starting Mode, (2) Direct-Coupling Mode, (3) Drag Mode, (4) Reverse Excitation Mode, (5) Zero Mode.

469.title: FUNCTION
469.body:
FUNCTION AT-12 A self-diagnostic function has been programmed into the ECU. Seven circuits can be checked for possible faults using the N·CVT warning light to access the problem area.
Consult can also be used for diagnostics. Refer to the Trouble Diagnosis section for diagnostic procedures. A synchromesh changeover system allows for forward to reverse shifting, at the same time eliminating "shift squeak" (occurs when the belt tries to reverse direction very quickly.) The synchroniser sleeve is central on the hub, and no power is transmitted. N Position synchromesh components Reverse idler gear Reverse driven gear Hub Sleeve Main drive gear Primary pulley Power Counter gear The synchroniser sleeve shifts to the right. Power is transmitted through the hub to the primary (input) pulley. D and Ds Position synchromesh components Power The synchroniser sleeve shifts to the left. Power is transmitted through the reverse idler gear and counter gear to the primary pulley. R Position synchromesh components Reverse idler gear Power Counter gear The synchroniser sleeve remains at the reverse position. The park rod and cam engage the park pawl with the park gear. The output shaft is mechanically locked. P Position park mechanism components Cam Parking pawl Parking gear Secondary pulley Applies to N·CVT (Continuously Variable Transmission) equipped Nissan Micra K11 N Position - synchroniser sleeve components: Reverse idler gear, Reverse driven gear, Hub, Sleeve, Main drive gear, Primary pulley, Power, Counter gear D and Ds Position - synchroniser sleeve shifted to right, Power arrow shown R Position - synchroniser sleeve shifted to left, Reverse idler gear, Power, Counter gear shown P Position - Cam, Parking pawl, Parking gear, Secondary pulley shown

470.title: FUNCTION — Belt and Pulley Mechanism
470.body: FUNCTION — Belt and Pulley Mechanism AT-13 The drives through a belt and pulley arrangement. This consists of a steel belt, an input (primary) pulley, and an output (secondary) pulley. The pulley groove widths can be varied in the axial direction, giving continuous (stepless) speed changes from low (ratio 2.5037) to overdrive (ratio 0.4977). Groove width is determined by hydraulic pressure acting upon a chamber in the sliding half of the pulley. Speed ratio — Low (r2/r1) 2.5037 Primary pulley groove narrow, Secondary pulley groove wide Speed ratio — Overdrive (r2/r1) 0.4977 Primary pulley groove wide, Secondary pulley groove narrow The steel drive belt consists of 280 steel plates supported by two laminated steel bands. When hydraulic pressure is applied to the output (secondary) pulley, the steel plates are forced upwards creating a tension at the bands. Power is therefore transmitted by compression of the steel blocks, with the laminated bands maintaining the necessary friction force. Steel band length (approx.) 600 (23.62) mm (in) Steel block count (approx.) 280 Belt cross-section angle 22 degrees Pulley sheave contact angle Belt width 24 (0.94) mm (in) Steel band width 9 (0.35) mm (in) Belt height 14.5 (0.571) mm (in) Steel block thickness 2 (0.08) mm (in) Steel band thickness 0.2 (0.008) mm (in) x10 laminated bands Both input (primary) and output (secondary) pulleys consist of a fixed sheave and a sliding sheave. The slope of the sheave is 11 degrees. A hydraulic chamber is located at the rear of the sliding sheave. The sheave moves on a ball splined shaft. Applies to CVT (Continuously Variable Transmission) equipped Nissan Micra K11. Speed ratio range: low 2.5037 to overdrive 0.4977. Belt and pulley mechanism diagram showing Low ratio (r2/r1 = 2.5037) and Overdrive ratio (r2/r1 = 0.4977) positions of primary (input) and secondary (output) pulleys with narrow/wide groove labels. Steel belt cross-sectional view showing dimensions (22°, 24 (0.94), 9 (0.35), 14.5 (0.571), 2 (0.08), 0.2 (0.008) x 10), steel band length approx. 600 (23.62), and steel block count approx. 280.

471.title: FUNCTION — Reduction Gear System
471.body: FUNCTION — Reduction Gear System AT-14 The reduction gear system consists of a reduction gear and a final drive gear. Gears are helical, and a speedometer drive is located on the differential case. The differential is of similar construction to the manual transaxle. Cross-section view of reduction gear system components Reduction gear Final drive gear Speedometer drive gear Automatic transaxle (AT) section. Differential described as similar construction to manual transaxle. Cross-section diagram of the reduction gear system showing reduction gear, final drive gear, and speedometer drive gear locations on the differential case

472.title: OPERATION — PERFORMANCE CHARACTERISTICS
472.body:
OPERATION — PERFORMANCE CHARACTERISTICS AT-15 The speed change characteristic of the is not linear (like that of a manual transmission vehicle, but can be expressed as the region bounded by the maximum and minimum pulley ratios.
In the graph, the MAX line shows the relationship between engine speed and vehicle speed at full acceleration: the minimum line shows the same relationship when accelerating slowly. Selector lever function "D" Position.
Normal driving mode, from starting off to maximum speed.
Speed change occurs between 1600 and 4000rev/min depending upon gradual or fast acceleration.
When cruising speed is reached and pressure on the accelerator pedal is reduced (transmission enters overdrive), the pulley ratio is at maximum.
If the pedal is pressed while cruising – ie to accelerate – the pulley ratio shifts to minimum and vehicle speed rises quickly. "Ds" Position.
In the "Ds" mode, the speed (ratio) range is limited to the higher zone of the speed change region. "Ds" also provides an engine braking effect. "R" Position.
In the reverse mode, the pulleys are locked in "low", and the speed (ratio) change does not occur. "P" Position.
In the park position, the output (secondary) pulley is locked mechanically. "N" Position.
In this position, the forward and reverse changeover is locked in a neutral state. Speed change range (D position) 1600 and 4000 rev/min depending upon gradual or fast acceleration Applies to N.CVT (Nissan CVT) equipped K11 Micra. CVT speed change characteristic compared with manual transmission gear speeds (1st–5th speed, overdrive). Ds range provides limited higher-zone speed change region with engine braking. Two graphs showing CVT speed change characteristics. Left graph: Engine revolution (rpm) vs Vehicle speed (km/h) comparing N.CVT (solid line, MAX and MIN bands) with Manual transmission gear speeds (1st through 5th, overdrive, dashed lines). Right graph: Engine revolution (rpm) vs Vehicle speed showing MAX line, Ds range region, Ds (MIN) and D (MIN) lines.

473.title: OPERATION - HYDRAULIC SYSTEM
473.body: OPERATION - HYDRAULIC SYSTEM AT-16 The hydraulic system basically consists of: Hydraulic System Components Oil pump driven by the engine. Hydraulic control valve to control oil pressure and speed change. Input signals relating to accelerator position, pulley ratio and engine speed. Shift lock safety mechanism. Oil cooler. The oil pump is driven by the engine via a shaft passing through the primary pulley. The pump is of the external gear type. As well as providing line pressure to operate the control valve, the pump also lubricates the drive belt by spraying oil (at high pressure) directly on to it. The pulley ratio sensor is also lubricated in the same manner. 475.title: OPERATION
475.body:
OPERATION AT-18 The hydraulic control valve assembly consists of:
1) Pressure regulator valve.
2) Shift control valve.
3) Engine brake valve. Pressure regulator valve schematic showing all circuits and components 1 - Pressure regulator valve (circuit/spring area) 2 - Valve body area 3 - Chamber 3 4 - Line pressure control solenoid valve 5 - Circuit 5 6 - Drain circuit 6 (X = Drain) 7 - Pitot pressure circuit D1 - Spool valve D1 D2 - Spool valve D2 Spring 1 Primary pulley sliding sheave Sensor shoe Pulley ratio (Large) - arrow left Pulley ratio (Small) - arrow right Pitot pressure Line pressure Secondary pulley Oil pump Shift control valve Lubricating oil pressure X = Drain The pressure regulator valve ensures that optimum hydraulic pressure is maintained at the secondary (output) pulley and determines secondary pulley groove width according to primary pulley groove width. Pressure in circuits 1 and 2 is balanced by spring 1. Pressure is determined by speed ratio, engine rev/min and a torque signal from the ECU. The pulley ratio sensor detects the position of the primary pulley and alters the regulator valve spring length accordingly. The speed of the pulley is sensed and pitot pressure (proportional to primary pulley rev/min) acts upon circuit 7 opposite the spring. When primary pulley (engine) rev/min increases, pitot pressure also increases, and the pressure regulator valve moves to the right to prevent line pressure increasing (due to increased oil pump speed). When it becomes necessary to change from low to high pressure (or vice versa), line pressure is delivered to chamber 3 from the control solenoid valve 4 via circuit 5. Under partial load conditions, line pressure is controlled by the solenoid valve 4 (ON) and the difference in diameter of the spool valves D1 and D2. Diameter D2 is larger than D1, working area is increased, line pressure is reduced. Under heavy load conditions, the solenoid valve 4 (OFF) closes and shuts off the oil pressure chamber lines. Pressure from circuit 5 is drained via circuit 6. Line pressure increases. Applies to CVT (Continuously Variable Transmission) equipped K11 Nissan Micra models. Pressure regulator valve diagram showing primary pulley sliding sheave, sensor shoe, spring, spool valves D1 and D2, line pressure control solenoid valve (4), pitot pressure circuit (7), line pressure, secondary pulley, oil pump, shift control valve, lubricating oil pressure, and drain points (X). Circuits numbered 1-7 are labeled.

477.title: OPERATION
477.body: OPERATION AT-20 The shift control valve controls continuous speed changes from low to overdrive in "D" and "Ds" modes. This valve controls the pulley ratio by controlling the flow of line pressure into the primary pulley according to input signals from the accelerator position and engine speed. In low, the shift control valve is positioned to the left. As engine speed increases, pitot pressure moves the control valve to the right, and line pressure is directed to the primary pulley via circuit. As the pulley ratio changes from low to overdrive, a "rise characteristic" increases engine speed to the required rev/min. This is achieved by the modulator linkage connecting the pulley ratio sensor to the shift control valve and compensation plunger. The pin and spring 4 prevent sudden draining of hydraulic pressure from the primary pulley by slowing down the movement of the shift control valve when the accelerator pedal is pressed suddenly. A shift lock valve also prevents oil draining from the primary pulley circuit. The engine brake valve enables the engine to maintain a comparatively high speed during deceleration when the selector lever is in the "Ds" position. In "D" position, the engine brake plunger blocks the oil passage, pressure is directed to the engine brake valve, valve spring force is overcome and the valve spool moves to the right. D range (OVERDRIVE) hydraulic circuit diagram Primary pulley Belt lubrication Secondary pulley Primary pressure Check valve Oil cooler Oil pump Oil filter Lubricating valve Line pressure Lubricating oil pressure Line pressure control solenoid valve Check valve Shift control valve Pitot pressure Spring Shift lock valve Regulator valve Modulator linkage Sensor shoe Ds modifier lever Plunger (Engine brake) Shift cam Spool Engine brake valve Pitot supply X = Drain Applies to CVT (automatic transmission) equipped Nissan Micra K11. Selector positions: R, N, D, Ds. Hydraulic circuit diagram for D range (OVERDRIVE) showing primary pulley, secondary pulley, belt lubrication, oil pump, oil cooler, oil filter, check valve, lubricating valve, line pressure control solenoid valve, regulator valve, modulator linkage, shift control valve, engine brake valve, shift lock valve, sensor shoe, Ds modifier lever, plunger (engine brake), shift cam, pitot pressure, spring, spool, and associated oil passages. X = Drain.

478.title: OPERATION
478.body: OPERATION AT-21 In "Ds" position, the plunger allows the circuit to drain, the engine brake valve spool is overcome by spring pressure and moves to the left. The engine brake valve arm, therefore, moves the shift control valve to the left, regardless of the position of the shift cam. Ds range hydraulic circuit diagram showing all valves, pulleys, and pressure lines in operation Primary pulley Belt lubrication Secondary pulley Primary pressure Check valve Oil cooler Oil pump (P) Oil filter Lubricating valve Line pressure Shift lock valve Lubricating oil pressure Line pressure control solenoid valve Check valve Shift control valve Pitot pressure Spring Engine brake valve Spool Regulator valve Modulator linkage Sensor shoe Ds modifier lever Plunger (Engine brake) Shift cam Pilot supply X = Drain This situation maintains pressure at the primary pulley, ie does not allow the shift cam to control pulley ratio variation, and so, as the vehicle speed decreases, it "pulls down" the engine speed with it, giving an engine braking effort. Applies to Nissan Micra K11 automatic transmission (CVT) Ds range engine braking operation. Hydraulic circuit diagram for Ds range operation showing primary pulley, secondary pulley, oil pump, oil cooler, oil filter, lubricating valve, line pressure control solenoid valve, check valves, shift control valve, engine brake valve, spool, shift cam, shift lock valve, regulator valve, modulator linkage, Ds modifier lever, plunger (engine brake), sensor shoe, and associated pressure lines including pilot supply, belt lubrication, lubricating oil pressure, primary pressure, line pressure, and pitot pressure. X = Drain is indicated.

479.title: OPERATION
479.body: OPERATION AT-22 A pitot tube is inserted into a chamber attached to the primary pulley, and a hydraulic pressure is detected and interpreted by the control valve assembly as engine speed. Pitot pressure chamber assembly on primary pulley Pitot pressure Oil rotates with pulley Fixed to case Integral with primary pulley Chamber If all four wheels lock up under braking, pitot pressure is not produced and the pulley (speed) ratio will change. The mechanism "holds" the pulley ratio as it was when lock-up occurred. This prevents the engine overreving. An oil cooler is located as part of the vehicle radiator. Oil is fed to the cooler under pressure, where its temperature is reduced by the radiator oil flow. 491.title: ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Control Valve Assembly
491.body: ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Control Valve Assembly AT-34 Removal Drain A.T.F. from transaxle. Remove oil pan and gasket. Disconnect control cable. Remove oil strainer and O ring. There may be a large quantity of oil within the strainer and inlet port. Have a container ready. Disconnect the harness. There may be a large quantity of oil within the strainer and inlet port. Have a container ready. A/T (automatic transaxle) procedure Oil pan on transaxle, with arrow indicating removal direction Control cable end connection on transaxle Oil strainer and gasket area after oil pan removal Control valve assembly exposed after strainer removal Harness disconnection from control valve assembly

492.title: ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Control Valve Assembly
492.body: ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Control Valve Assembly AT-35 Removal (Cont'd) Remove the spring clip and disconnect the modulator rod. Fit Tool to control valve to prevent the pressure regulator valve assembly jumping out. Fit the regulator end of the holder first, then press down at the opposite end and locate the holder around the centre bolt. Remove the valve and holder as a unit. Take care the valve and holder do not seperate. Have a container ready to catch any residual oil in the valve. Do not disassemble the control valve. Note: Have a container ready to catch any residual oil in the valve. Do not disassemble the control valve. Ensure all components are clean & free from dirt and contamination. Installation Fit the valve and holder. Ensure the dowel is correctly located. Shows spring clip, rod, bolt, and plate on the modulator rod connection area Shows tool KV319K0100 fitted to control valve to prevent pressure regulator valve assembly jumping out Shows control valve assembly in position on vehicle Shows removal of valve and holder as a unit from the transmission Shows installation of valve and holder with dowel location during installation step

493.title: ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Control Valve Assembly — Installation (Cont'd)
493.body: ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Control Valve Assembly — Installation (Cont'd) AT-36 Installation (Cont'd) Temporarily tighten the valve bolts. Ensure that the engine brake lever is in contact with the ratio sensor bolt. Press on the valve body to ensure correct location, and tighten the bolts. Connect the modulator rod. Fit Tool plate as shown. Slacken off the adjuster bolt. Push the adjuster plate in the direction of the arrow, and tighten the bolt to remove free play. Remove the tool. Connect the harness & control cable. Fit the oil strainer (using a new O ring). Fit the oil pan using a new gasket. Fill with new ATF. Adjuster bolt torque 2.9 - 3.9 N·m Step 13 — tighten adjuster bolt Adjuster bolt torque 0.3 - 0.4 kg·m Step 13 — tighten adjuster bolt Adjuster bolt torque 2.2 - 2.9 ft·lb Step 13 — tighten adjuster bolt Engine brake lever in contact with ratio sensor bolt on valve body assembly Modulator rod connection with tool KV319K0030 fitted and adjuster bolt location Adjuster plate being pushed in direction of arrow with tool KV319K0030 fitted; torque spec shown as 2.9 - 3.9 N·m (0.3 - 0.4 kg·m, 2.2 - 2.9 ft·lb)

494.title: ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Throttle Wire
494.body: ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Throttle Wire AT-37 Check that the throttle wire stroke length (L) is within the standard limit when the throttle drum moves from idle (P2) to wide open (P1). Standard stroke length (L) 51.2 +- 2 mm Throttle drum from idle (P2) to wide open (P1) Adjustment Turn ignition switch "OFF". While pressing on the lock plate, return the adjustment tube all the way to the transaxle side. This movement locks the adjustment tube. Make sure the adjustment tube is locked correctly and release the lock plate. Set the throttle drum in fully open position (P1). Then the adjustment tube presses the lock plate down, and the tube should return to the throttle drum side. The adjustment is completed where the adjustment tube stops. The adjustment tube should stop where the inner wire tension and the reaction of the return spring produce the same force. The throttle drum can also be opened wide as described below. Make sure the throttle drum is fully open when the accelerator pedal is fully open. (Quickly press the accelerator pedal to the fully open position). 1. Perform the adjustment procedure described in items 1 to 4 above, not only when the throttle wire is first connected, but also when engine throttle system is adjusted or the throttle cable is reassembled. 2. There should be no play in the cable on the N-CVT side because the throttle wire is adjusted automatically. Removal - Transaxle side Remove drain plug from oil pan to drain A/T fluid. Remove oil pan and gasket. Always replace the oil pan gasket with a new one. Do not reuse. Remove wire end from control valve. Remove 6mm bolt from throttle wire. Procedure applies to N-CVT (automatic) transaxle. Throttle wire adjusted automatically on N-CVT side. Throttle wire adjuster assembly diagram showing throttle drum positions P1 and P2, outer cable, adjuster tube, bracket, lock plate, spring, adjuster body, and stroke length L Engine bay view showing throttle wire routing on transaxle side Close-up diagram of throttle wire connection at transaxle showing cable, bolt, and wire end locations

495.title: ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Throttle Wire
495.body:
ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Throttle Wire AT-38 Make sure the inner wire of wire assembly moves smoothly. Make sure there is no wear or damage. Check that outer wire connection points are not loose. Check that there is no wear or damage on outer wire. Installation Fit a new O-ring (7 x 1.9) to the wire assembly and insert in transaxle case hole. Tighten bolt and washer. Pass inner wire through casing and control valve guide grove. Install wire end. Pull inner wire from outside the unit. Make sure the inner cable operates smoothly inside the case and does not contact the case wall and valve body assembly. Align throttle wire plastic casing mating mark A with engine bracket mating mark B. Assemble throttle wire plastic casing in engine bracket. After assembly, make sure part C is expanded and installed securely as shown in view X of figure. Install the throttle wire in throttle drum and charging pipe bracket. Adjust throttle wire length (refer to Throttle Wire Adjustment) and secure band and clip. Note:
1. Do not apply excessive force to the wire.
2. If the inner wire is bent, straighten it before installation.
3. When installing inner wire in throttle drum, do not use long-nose pliers or other tools to prevent the wire from bending. Applicable to automatic transaxle (AT) equipped vehicles. Inner cable shown at engine side and transaxle side of throttle wire assembly Inner cable routing through transaxle case area Wire end, throttle valve, cable, and guide groove inside the transaxle control valve body Throttle drum, adjuster tube, bracket, lock plate, adjuster body, and mating marks A and B; also view C showing lock plate expanded

496.title: ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Inhibitor Switch
496.body: ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Inhibitor Switch AT-39 Adjustment Remove the snap pin, plain washer and conical washer from the A/T assembly select linkage. Disconnect the control linkage. Set the manual shaft of the A/T, to the "N" position. The "N" position is the location where the inhibitor switch adjustment hole and manual shaft adjustment hole are aligned. Loosen the three inhibitor switch bolts. Insert pin (4 mm diameter) into adjustment holes in both inhibitor switch and manual shaft of A/T assembly. Tighten the three bolts to the specified torque. Install the parts that were removed. Check continuity of the inhibitor switch. (Refer to "Trouble Diagnosis", Electrical Component Inspection.) A/T assembly select linkage area showing inhibitor switch and manual shaft alignment holes Cable Select linkage Hole of manual shaft Hole of inhibitor switch Inhibitor switch Manual shaft Close-up of inhibitor switch showing alignment hole and bolt locations Inhibitor switch Alignment hole Bolts Procedure applies to A/T (Automatic Transmission) equipped vehicles View of A/T assembly showing cable, select linkage, hole of manual shaft, hole of inhibitor switch, inhibitor switch, and manual shaft Close-up view of inhibitor switch showing alignment hole and bolts used during adjustment procedure

497.title: ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Control Linkage
497.body: ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Control Linkage AT-40 Inspection Move the selector lever and make sure it does not catch or make any abnormal noise. Move the selector lever from the "P" range to the "Ds" range. The detents in each range should be felt. If the detents cannot be felt or the pointer indicating the range is improperly aligned, the cable needs adjustment. Procedures to shift into each operation position are shown in the figure. Ensure that reversing lamp lights and warning buzzer sounds in reverse gear. Ensure the engine can only be started in "P" and "N" range. Check the transmission is locked completely in "P" range. Selector lever shift pattern showing P, R, N, D, Ds positions Operation of release button required Operation of release button not required Adjustment Slacken off lock nuts "A" and "B". Move selector lever to the "P" range. Move the transaxle select lever to the "P" range. Ensure transmission is locked. Hold the rod front end and cable adjustment screw at a right angle and run the lock nuts up to each side of the rod plate. Hold lock nut "A" while applying final torque tightening to "B". Lock nut tightening torque 12 to 15 (1.2 to 1.5) N-m (kg-m) Lock nuts A and B on control linkage rod plate Note: Do not move the select lever or inner rod during adjustment. Applies to automatic transmission (A/T) control linkage adjustment only. Selector lever shift pattern diagram showing P, R, N, D, Ds positions with arrows indicating when release button operation is required or not required Control linkage adjustment diagram showing cable, lock nut A, lock nut B, and rod components

498.title: ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Vehicle Speed Sensor
498.body: ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Vehicle Speed Sensor AT-41 Replacement Remove the vehicle speed sensor (output shaft speed sensor) from the A/T. Always replace the O-ring with a new one. Do not reuse. Always replace the O-ring with a new one. Do not reuse. Procedure applies to A/T (automatic transmission) equipped vehicles. Illustration showing the vehicle speed sensor (output shaft speed sensor) location on the A/T unit, with the sensor callout label pointing to the component.

499.title: ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Accelerator and Accelerator position Switch
499.body: ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Accelerator and Accelerator position Switch AT-42 Assembled condition Check that the bolts of the accelerator and accelerator position switch are tightened securely. Refer to Electrical Component Inspection, "Trouble Diagnosis" for inspection of the accelerator and accelerator position switch. Operation Check that switch moves within standard distance (stroke) when accelerator pedal is released. Accelerator switch operation stroke Approx. 3.0 mm When accelerator pedal is released Accelerator position switch operation stroke Approx. 17.5 mm When accelerator pedal is released Diagram showing the accelerator switch and accelerator position switch mounted on the accelerator pedal assembly Diagram showing the accelerator pedal assembly at released position with operation stroke measurements: 17.5 mm for accelerator position switch and 3.0 mm for accelerator switch

500.title: ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Brush Holder
500.body: ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Brush Holder AT-43 Removal Disconnect brush holder connector. Remove two bolts securing brush holder. Move brush holder slightly to the right and raise to remove it. Note: Do not bend the brush when removing it from the brush holder. Inspect the brush wear distance. Replace the brush if the brush end gets close to the brush limit mark position. Note: When determining whether to replace or continue use of a brush, consider if the brush will last until the next inspection of the brush. Observe the following points when replacing the brush. If the contact area of the brush is scratched, inspect the slip ring because foreign matter may become attached to it. (Refer to Removal and Installation for slip ring cleaning procedure.) Check for loose bolts. Check the continuity between the harness and the terminal (5 mm screw) and the insulation between the harness and bracket. Before separating the engine and transaxle, remove the brush holder from the case to prevent damage. Be careful not to bend or damage the brush when removing the brush holder from the case. Do not move the brush up and down unnecessarily with fingertip. This may cause the lead wire to disconnect. When there is oil on the brush, check for oil leaks and repair as necessary. Replace the brush holder and electromagnetic clutch if required. On-vehicle photo showing brush holder location with arrow indicating position Illustration of brush holder showing brush limit mark position Illustration showing incorrect practice — bending brush — marked with X

501.title: ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Brush Holder
501.body:
ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Brush Holder AT-44 Installation Insert the brush holder straight into the hole on upper side of housing and tighten the two bolts. Tightening torque 6.0 to 8.0 N-m Tightening torque 0.6 to 0.8 kg-m Note:
(1) When the transmission has been removed from the engine, install the brush holder after the transmission is reinstalled.
(2) Be careful when inserting the brush holder. Excessive force may damage the brush connection surface and brush sliding surface. Photograph showing the brush holder location on the transmission housing, with the brush holder indicated by a label and arrow.

502.title: ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Differential Side Oil Seal
502.body: ON VEHICLE SERVICE — Differential Side Oil Seal AT-45 Removal Remove drive shaft from vehicle. Refer to FA, "Drive Shaft". Pry off the differential side oil seal with a screwdriver. Always replace the oil seal with a new part. Do not reuse. Be careful not to scratch the case. Installation Using a drift, insert oil seal so it conforms to the following dimensions. Installation Standards — RE0F05A Model Part Drift dimensions Installation dimensions Right side Outer dia. 56 mm, Inner dia. 46 mm Level difference with case and surface = 0.5 mm max. Left side Outer dia. 56 mm, Inner dia. 46 mm Level difference with case and surface = 0.5 mm max. RE0F05A model. Right side and left side oil seal drift dimensions are the same: Outer dia. 56 mm, Inner dia. 46 mm. Installation level difference with case surface = 0.5 mm max. for both sides. Illustration showing the differential side oil seal location on the vehicle, with the seal labeled Diagram showing left side and right side of differential with 0.5 mm (max) installation dimension indicators for oil seal level difference

505.title: TROUBLE DIAGNOSIS
505.body: TROUBLE DIAGNOSIS AT-48 Basic inspection and adjustment is usually enough to deal with N-CVT problems. Therefore, before starting detailed troubleshooting, perform the preliminary checks below. For trouble diagnosis, first determine where the primary cause of trouble is, such as in the clutch system, control unit, switches, the hydraulic control valve or transaxle main unit. Preliminary Check Check all engine adjustments are correct. In particular, check idle speed, intake compression at idle, and ignition timing. Check that the accelerator pedal, N-CVT throttle wire, and linkage are correct. (Check the fully open and fully closed functions.) Check that the accelerator and accelerator position switches are set correctly (pedal stroke). Adjust the control linkage and check that unit connectors and inhibitor switch operation points are set correctly. Check that N-CVT control unit and brush holder connectors are attached securely. Check for oil leaks. Check that A/T fluid level is correct. Refer to "On-Vehicle Service" for the adjustment procedures described above. N-CVT (Nissan Continuously Variable Transmission) specific procedure

506.title: Trouble Diagnosis — Preliminary Check (Cont'd) — Self Diagnosis
506.body: Trouble Diagnosis — Preliminary Check (Cont'd) — Self Diagnosis AT-49 The N-CVT warning lamp normally lights when the electrical system malfunctions and a problem is detected. The N-CVT warning lamp blinks if the electromagnetic clutch coil temperature rises excessively, or the motor stalls for a long period of time. The lamp goes off if the temperature lowers or the engine stall stops. Refer to "Quick and Accurate Trouble Diagnosis" when the lamp blinks frequently. In models equipped with ABS, turn ignition switch "ON", turn on power to CONSULT and touch "START". When the engine runs, the N-CVT warning lamp lights and self-diagnosis displayed indicates that the ABS operation signal path circuit is damaged. This is not the case, it is for test purpose only. Self Diagnosis Flowchart (A) A fault occurs (see note 1) Perform self diagnosis (see note 2) Perform test drive (see note 3) Perform self diagnosis (see note 4) Does self diagnosis indicate problem? Check other troubles. Refer to "Diagnostic Procedures" Repair/Replace malfunctioning parts Perform test drive: Drive at full acceleration up to 50 km/h, then constant speed for 5 seconds. Decelerate to 30 km/h without pressing the brake pedal Any fault occurs? End No Yes Yes No Note 1: The N-CVT warning lamp lights when there is trouble in the electrical system. After the ignition switch is turned "OFF" and engine is started again, N-CVT warning lamp goes off. Note 2: Check problem that occurred during driving. Record diagnosis results. Note 3: Verify problem condition. Note 4: Check problem that occurred during driving test. Record inspection results. ABS-equipped models: N-CVT warning lamp behaviour during CONSULT test is for test purpose only and does not indicate actual ABS signal path damage. N-CVT Warning Lamp operation timing diagrams — showing OK (normal) and Fault (blinking) lamp behaviour relative to IGN-SW ON and Engine start events over time

507.title: Trouble Diagnosis — Self-diagnosis Items (using CONSULT)
507.body: Trouble Diagnosis — Self-diagnosis Items (using CONSULT) AT-50 Self-diagnosis Items (using CONSULT) Turn ignition switch "OFF" Connect CONSULT connector to vehicle diagnostic connector. The diagnostic connector is in the fuse box. Start engine. Touch "START", "CVT", "SELF-DIAG RESULTS" in this order on the CONSULT screen. Self diagnostic results are displayed on the screen. Note: Take the following precautions when using CONSULT for self-diagnosis of ENGINE and N•CVT in models equipped with ABS. Connect the vehicle self-diagnostic connector to CONSULT and turn the ignition switch "ON". When the "SELF-DIAG RESULTS" screen is displayed, the ABS warning lamp blinks, the ABS self-diagnosis result screen starts and the fail-safe mode is entered. The conditions described above occur when self-diagnosis is performed with CONSULT. When each self-diagnosis is completed, always turn ignition switch "OFF" to cancel the fail-safe. Applicable to models equipped with N•CVT and ABS. Note regarding ABS warning lamp behaviour when using CONSULT for ENGINE and N•CVT self-diagnosis on ABS-equipped models. Illustration of the CONSULT diagnostic tool device CONSULT screen showing NISSAN CONSULT with START and SUB-MODE buttons CONSULT screen showing SELF-DIAG RESULTS with FAILURE DETECTED RANGE SWITCH and ERASE/PRINT buttons

508.title: Trouble Diagnosis — Self-diagnosis Items (using CONSULT) (Cont'd) — Self diagnosis display chart / Data monitor chart
508.body:
Trouble Diagnosis — Self-diagnosis Items (using CONSULT) (Cont'd) — Self diagnosis display chart / Data monitor chart AT-51 Self diagnosis display chart Trouble Description Remarks Vehicle speed sensor Vehicle speed sensor is not input due to disconnection  Range (inhibitor) switch system Two or more range switches input simultaneously due to a short-circuit in the range switch main unit or signal circuit  Accelerator switch or accelerator position switch The accelerator switch and accelerator position switch interface is incorrect due to a short-circuit in the switch, a signal short-circuit or disconnection.  Torque signal Torque signal is not input correctly due to signal short-circuit or disconnection ON/OFF signal is output from ECCS C/U when starting ABS operation signal The ABS signal is not input correctly due to a short-circuit in ABS operation signal system or disconnection
Rated voltage is not applied to relay due to a short in idle-up relay output circuit or disconnection ON/OFF signal is output from ABS C/U when starting. On models equipped with ABS, turn ignition switch "ON". Turn on CONSULT and touch "START". Start engine N-CVT warning lamp lights and self-diagnosis display indicates that the ABS operation signal path circuit is damaged. In this case however, it is not damaged. Electro-magnetic clutch Current does not flow correctly to clutch coil due to a short circuit in the output circuit, disconnection or clutch trouble  Line pressure solenoid The rated voltage is not applied to the solenoid due to short in output circuit or disconnection.  Data monitor chart ITEM Unit REMARKS Vehicle speed sensor (km/h)  Engine rpm (rpm)  Clutch reference current (A) 0 amps is displayed in reverse magnetization mode Clutch actual current (A) 0 amps is displayed in reverse magnetization mode Pulley ratio  Signal quality is poor below 20 km/h (12 mph) Accelerator switch (ON/OFF display)  (Idle switch) Accelerator position switch (ON/OFF display)  Accelerator position: approx. 17.5 mm where wire attaches "ON" turns "OFF" Air conditioner signal (ON/OFF display)   Stop lamp switch (ON/OFF display)   R range switch (ON/OFF display)   D range switch (ON/OFF display)   Ds range switch (ON/OFF display)   Neutral switch (ON/OFF display)   ABS operation signal (ON/OFF display)  Also displayed in models not equipped with ABS Torque signal (ON/OFF display)  "OFF" when torque is high (line pressure increases) Engine temperature signal (ON/OFF display)  "ON" at low engine temperature Idle up solenoid (ON/OFF display)  "ON" when ABS operates. (Also displayed on models not equipped with ABS) Clutch signal (ON/OFF display)  "ON" when connected Line pressure solenoid (ON/OFF display)  "OFF" when line pressure is high Refers to "CONSULT Operation Manual" for further information. ABS operation signal notes distinguish between models equipped with ABS and those not equipped with ABS. Idle up solenoid also displayed on models not equipped with ABS.

509.title: Self Diagnosis Items (without using CONSULT)
509.body:
Self Diagnosis Items (without using CONSULT) AT-52 Self Diagnosis Items (without using CONSULT) Start self diagnosis Park vehicle on a flat surface A
Release accelerator pedal (accelerator switch and accelerator position switch are "ON") and move selector lever to "D" range Turn ignition switch on (do not start engine) Does N-CVT warning lamp light? Go to N-CVT warning lamp circuit inspection B
Depress brake pedal (stop lamp switch goes "ON" and fully depress accelerator pedal (accelerator switch and accelerator position switch go "OFF") Move selector lever in following order:
D → Ds → D → N → R → P C
Release accelerator pedal (accelerator switch and accelerator position switch go "ON") and start the engine Count number of N-CVT warning lamp blinks. Refer to "Self-diagnosis results" on next page End self diagnosis procedure No Yes Applicable to N-CVT (Nissan CVT) automatic transmission variant of the K11 Micra Illustration showing step A: releasing accelerator pedal and moving selector lever to D range Illustration showing step B: depressing brake pedal and fully depressing accelerator pedal, with selector lever positions N, R, P, D, Ds shown Illustration showing step C: releasing accelerator pedal and starting the engine

510.title: Trouble Diagnosis — Self Diagnosis Items (without using CONSULT) (Cont'd)
510.body: Trouble Diagnosis — Self Diagnosis Items (without using CONSULT) (Cont'd) AT-53 Self Diagnosis Blink Pattern Reference N-CVT warning lamp Faulty Circuit Start diagnosis: 7 blinks. Starting signal ON then OFF. t1=2 secs, t2=2-5 secs, t3=1 sec. All circuits confirmed OK. 1. When first blink is longer than others Accelerator or accelerator position switch circuit is short-circuited or disconnected. Accel. sw. terminals 1,2,3,4 and Accel. position sw. connected to ECU terminals 6 and 15. Go to accelerator and accelerator position switch circuit check. 2. When second blink is longer than others Inhibitor switch circuit is short-circuited or disconnected. IGN-SW — 10A — Inhibit. sw. positions P(7), R(8), N(9), D(6), DS(5) connected to ECU terminals 10, 5, 2, 3. Go to inhibit switch circuit check. 3. When third blink is longer than others Vehicle speed sensor is short-circuited or disconnected. Vehicle speed sensor terminals 2 and 1 connected to ECU terminals 7 and 16. Go to vehicle speed sensor circuit check. t1 2 secs Starting signal duration t2 2-5 secs ON period between blinks t3 1 sec Blink duration Self Diagnosis Blink Pattern Diagnosis Start diagnosis — 7 blinks (all circuits OK) First blink longer than others? Inhibitor switch circuit is short-circuited or disconnected Go to inhibit switch circuit check Third blink longer than others? 511.title: Trouble Diagnosis — Self Diagnosis Items (without using CONSULT) (Cont'd)
511.body: Trouble Diagnosis — Self Diagnosis Items (without using CONSULT) (Cont'd) AT-54 Self Diagnosis Blink Code Descriptions N-CVT warning lamp Faulty Circuit 4. When fourth blink is longer than others Electromagnetic clutch coil circuit is disconnected or short-circuited. 5. When fifth blink is longer than others Line pressure solenoid circuit is disconnected or short-circuited. 6. When sixth blink is longer than others Torque signal circuit is disconnected or short-circuited. 7. When seventh blink is longer than others A.B.S. signal or idle-up relay circuit is disconnected or short-circuited. Blink 4 — Electromagnetic Clutch Coil Circuit Brush holder E.C.U. Go to electromagnetic clutch coil circuit check. Blink 5 — Line Pressure Solenoid Circuit Line pressure solenoid E.C.U. Go to line pressure solenoid circuit check. Blink 6 — Torque Signal Circuit ECCS C/U Torque signal wire E.C.U. Go to torque signal circuit check. Blink 7 — A.B.S. Signal or Idle-up Relay Circuit IGN-SW Fuse 10A (1) Fuse 10A (2) A/C relay Idle-up relay circuit Magnetic clutch E.C.U. F.I.C.D. solenoid ABS C/U Go to A.B.S. signal or idle-up relay circuit check. (See note:) Note: A.B.S. warning light will flash, this is test purposes only. N-CVT (Continuously Variable Transmission) self diagnosis without CONSULT tool. Blink codes 4–7 described. A.B.S. warning light will flash during blink 7 diagnosis — test purposes only. 512.title: Self Diagnosis Items (without using CONSULT) (Cont'd)
512.body:
Self Diagnosis Items (without using CONSULT) (Cont'd) AT-55 N-CVT Warning Lamp vs Faulty Circuit N-CVT warning lamp Faulty circuit Lamp blinks at 4 Hz repeatedly • Battery performance decreased.
• Battery has been disconnected for a long time.
• Battery has been connected incorrectly.
• Memory back-up circuit (E.C.U. pin 21) is short-circuited or disconnected.

• Note: Lamp blinks at 4 Hz during continuous diagnosis, but this is not abnormal. Lamp does not blink • Go to inhibit switch, accel. switch, accel. position switch, brake lamp switch, or engine speed signal circuit are disconnected or short-circuited. Otherwise, the E.C.U. is faulty.
• Go to inhibit switch, accelerator switch, accelerator position switch, brake lamp switch and engine speed signal circuit check. Lamp blinks at 4 Hz repeatedly: Battery performance decreased. Battery has been disconnected for a long time. Battery has been connected incorrectly. Memory back-up circuit (E.C.U. pin 21) is short-circuited or disconnected. Note: Lamp blinks at 4 Hz during continuous diagnosis, but this is not abnormal. Lamp does not blink: Go to inhibit switch, accel. switch, accel. position switch, brake lamp switch, or engine speed signal circuit are disconnected or short-circuited. Otherwise, the E.C.U. is faulty. Go to inhibit switch, accelerator switch, accelerator position switch, brake lamp switch and engine speed signal circuit check. Torque Signal Circuit Check AT-65 ECCS C/U to N-CVT C/U connection   Data Monitor Display (MONITOR / NO FAIL) Signal Status S-RANGE SW OFF D-RANGE SW OFF R-RANGE SW OFF NEUT SW ON A.B.S. SIGNAL OFF TORQUE SIG ON Torque Signal Circuit Check A — Check input signal

Press brake pedal.
Select "D".

• Check that "TORQUE SIGNAL" display is "ON" when accelerator pedal is released. Check that "OFF" blinks at Stall speed (1,700-2,300 rpm) after brake pedal is fully depressed and accelerator pedal is depressed.

527.title: TROUBLE DIAGNOSIS
527.body: TROUBLE DIAGNOSIS AT-70 Diagnostic Procedures Index Procedure Page Vehicle Does Not drive AT-71 Vehicle Creeps AT-73 Faulty Select Operation AT-75 Faulty Shifting AT-76 Shift Characteristics Inspection AT-77 Engine Races While Driving AT-82 Stall Test AT-83 Electrical Components Inspection AT-86

528.title: Trouble Diagnosis — Vehicle Does Not Drive
528.body: Trouble Diagnosis — Vehicle Does Not Drive AT-71 Symptom and Diagnosis Items Symptom Diagnosis items Engine races but vehicle does not drive. (Operation resembles selector lever being in "N" range) Accelerator switch is faulty | Manual control linkage incorrectly adjusted | Electromagnetic clutch circuit is faulty | Engine speed signal is faulty | N-CVT Control unit or N-CVT control unit power supply is faulty | Inhibitor switch is faulty (Refer to diagnosis chart on following page) Check Sequence — Vehicle Does Not Drive Perform self diagnosis Inspect and repair based on trouble code. When O.K. code or trouble code is not output, check N-CVT control unit power supply. Check selector cable adjustment (Refer to "ON-VEHICLE SERVICE) | Chart reference number: 1 Adjust and replace shifter arm or selector cable Check accelerator switch circuit | Chart reference number: 2 Check or replace accelerator switch | Check or repair harnesses connected to N-CVT control unit Check engine RPM signal circuit | Chart reference number: 3, 4 Check or repair harness or connectors Check inhibitor switch circuit for all ranges | Chart reference number: 4, 5, 6 Check or repair inhibitor switch harness or connectors Check electromagnetic clutch circuit | Chart reference number: 7, 8 Check or repair electromagnetic clutch main unit, brush holder or harness. Replace N-CVT control unit. (When replacing N-CVT control unit check that electro-magnetic clutch circuit is not short-circuited to ground.) Check or repair: 1. Selector position mechanism 2. Oil pump 3. Control valve 4. Belt and pulley mechanism N.G. O.K. N.G. O.K. N.G. O.K. N.G. O.K. N.G. O.K. N.G. O.K. N.G. Applies to N-CVT (continuously variable transmission) variant of Nissan Micra K11. Flowchart / check sequence for diagnosing 'Vehicle Does Not Drive' symptom on N-CVT transmission

529.title: TROUBLE DIAGNOSIS
529.body:
TROUBLE DIAGNOSIS AT-72 Diagnosis Chart No. Probable cause and damage symptoms Countermeasure 1 Incorrect adjustment Check and adjust 2 Accelerator switch abnormality (when accelerator switch stays "ON")
• After engine starts, vehicle does not move. When the accelerator position switch operates, vehicle can start moving above engine rpm of 1,100 in "D" or "Ds" range because fail-safe mode is set. • Check accelerator switch
• Check accelerator switch function
• Check harness to N-CVT control unit
• Replace accelerator switch 3 Engine ground is faulty
• Engine speed fluctuates and is not smooth • Check for the ground terminal tightness. 4 Connector contact is faulty
• Problem condition occurs intermittently or irregularly. This also occurs because of vehicle vibration.
Related connectors:
1. Inhibitor switch connectors
2. N-CVT control unit connectors
   ① Range signal
   ② Clutch circuit output
3. Brush holder connector in engine room • Check insertion condition of connectors (Terminal open or removed)
• Check terminal crimping for disconnection
• Replace terminal 5 Improper inhibitor switch contact. (Dust or debris adheres to contact point or contact point operation is faulty) • Check inhibitor switch
• Replace inhibitor switch 6 Inhibitor switch adjustment is incorrect • Adjust inhibitor switch 7 Brush holder is faulty:
• Harness disconnection, faulty sealing or short circuit
• Brush distortion
• Soiled brush or slip ring (oil leak or water damage)
• Foreign matter in brush end, brush wear

• The brush holder itself is usually not damaged. In most cases, chips or particles (iron/aluminium) in the clutch housing short-circuit the slip ring or brush, or oil and water leaks cause incorrect connection.

• When there is a slip ring or brush short-circuit, this causes N-CVT control unit damage which may result in improper selector operation, cause the engine to stop in the drive range or idle incorrectly. However, there is an N-CVT protection function which disconnects the clutch when a clutch short-circuit is detected. • Replace brush holder (except where there is a short circuit)
• Replace oil seal (oil leaks)
• Check or repair housing seal (when water has penetrated)
• Remove foreign matter from housing 8 Insufficient clutch torque capacity, incorrect clutch operation
• Burn damage (powder loses magnetic characteristics)
• The clutch is exposed to water or oil
• Powder leak or short-circuit (The vehicle may not start to move)

• When the capacity insufficiency is slight, the vehicle may be driven even though the clutch may slip often. The condition will become more severe and the vehicle will not move at all eventually • Perform the stall test to check for insufficient capacity
• Clutch replacement (insufficient capacity)
• Oil seal replacement (oil leak) N-CVT (Nissan Continuously Variable Transmission) specific. References D and Ds range selector positions.

530.title: Trouble Diagnosis — Vehicle Creeps
530.body:
Trouble Diagnosis — Vehicle Creeps AT-73 Symptom: The vehicle moves slowly when shifting to driving range from a complete standstill. (When accelerator pedal is released)
NOTE: It is normal to have an almost imperceptible creep or a slight creep (less than the creep of a conventional hydraulic A/T) Diagnosis item:
• Accelerator switch or accelerator position switch is faulty
• N-CVT control unit is faulty
• Electromagnetic clutch is faulty
• Insulation is faulty on harness between electromagnetic clutch and N-CVT control unit (Refer to diagnosis chart on following page) Vehicle Creeps — Check Sequence Check accelerator switch circuit
Chart reference number: 1 Repair or replace accelerator switch, accelerator position switch or harness Measure clutch current
Chart reference number: 2,3
[Note 1] Check or replace brush holder harness. Replace electromagnetic clutch Replace N-CVT control unit N.G. O.K. N.G. O.K. O.K. Note 1 — Measure clutch current Connect CONSULT and start engine Select "DATA MONITOR" Pull handbrake securely, depress brake pedal, shift selector lever from "N" to "D" range while idling. Read "ACTUAL CURRENT" value on screen. Standard value: 0.1 - 0.2 A Measure current while depressing and releasing the accelerator pedal after having fulfilled conditions mentioned under item 3. (Set accelerator switch ON and OFF) Clutch current — Accelerator pedal is released (Accelerator switch "ON") 0.1 - 0.2 A Selector in D, engine idling, handbrake applied, accelerator pedal released Clutch current — Accelerator pedal is depressed (Accelerator switch "OFF") Minimum 0.2 A Selector in D, engine idling, handbrake applied, accelerator pedal depressed N-CVT (automatic transmission variant). Creep behaviour note: slight creep less than conventional hydraulic A/T is normal. CONSULT tool connected in vehicle interior, showing the diagnostic connector location

531.title: TROUBLE DIAGNOSIS
531.body:
TROUBLE DIAGNOSIS AT-74 Diagnosis Chart No. Probable cause and symptoms Countermeasure 1 • Accelerator switch is faulty (Accelerator switch stays "OFF")
• Accelerator position switch is faulty (Accelerator switch stays "OFF")
Vehicle creeps because accelerator is released but accelerator switch is "OFF" (same condition as if pedal is depressed). • Check accelerator switch and accelerator position switch.
• Check functioning of accelerator switch and accelerator position switch
• Check N-CVT control unit harness 2 Clutch circuit negative (-) side is short-circuited
Vehicle creeps due to high drag current. Engine may stop when this occurs • Check electromagnetic clutch circuit 3 Excessive torque is transmitted by electromagnetic clutch
The electromagnetic clutch may be faulty. When this is the case, the influence of the stall speed and select operability can be checked. For this reason, when only vehicle creep occurs, it may be considered as a temporary condition until the powder wears in. • Replace electromagnetic clutch if part is faulty. N-CVT (Nissan Continuously Variable Transmission) specific diagnosis chart related to vehicle creep conditions.

532.title: Trouble Diagnosis — Faulty Select Operation
532.body:
Trouble Diagnosis — Faulty Select Operation AT-75 Symptom:
• Selector lever cannot be moved
• Selector lever movement is difficult Diagnosis item:
• Brush holder is faulty
• Lever and linkage are faulty
• Electromagnetic clutch is faulty Faulty Select Operation — Check Procedure Check brush holder circuit (Refer to "ON-VEHICLE SERVICE") Repair or replace brush holder, harness or connector Check selector lever and control linkage (Refer to "ON-VEHICLE SERVICE") Adjust or replace selector lever or control linkage Check inhibitor switch circuit Adjust or replace inhibitor switch
Repair or replace harness or connector Operate select lever after engine is stopped (operation is possible but with difficulty) Check or repair N-CVT control unit
• Select position mechanism Does engine crank and start in "N" range? Replace electromagnetic clutch N.G. O.K. N.G. O.K. N.G. O.K. N.G. O.K. N.G. O.K. Applies to N-CVT (automatic transmission) variant of Nissan Micra K11.

533.title: Trouble Diagnosis — Faulty Shifting
533.body: Trouble Diagnosis — Faulty Shifting AT-76 Always perform the following preliminary checks before starting troubleshooting procedures when shift operation is faulty. Note: The term "faulty shifting" indicates a significant variance from the shift characteristics. Faulty Shifting Diagnostic Flowchart Check N-CVT fluid level Replenish N-CVT fluid (Check transmission main unit) Note: The transmission main unit may be damaged when shifting is faulty due to insufficient N-CVT fluid Check stall speed. Refer to "Stall test" Check the electromagnetic clutch, N-CVT and brush holder Check throttle wire & throttle wire adjustment Adjust or replace the throttle wire Check control linkage Adjust control linkage Check shift characteristics N.G. O.K. N.G. O.K. N.G. O.K. N.G. O.K. Applicable to N-CVT (automatic) transmission variant of K11 Nissan Micra.

534.title: Shift Characteristics Inspection
534.body: Shift Characteristics Inspection AT-77 The following chart indicates problems related to engine speed, speedometer and accelerator position based on the shift characteristic figure during driving or when the vehicle wheels are lifted off the ground in D, Ds range. Shift Characteristics Problem Chart No PROBLEM SHIFT CHARACTERISTIC FIGURE AREA ① Engine speed increases but vehicle speed does not increase accordingly. (Transmission does not shift out of low range) Line A ② Engine speed does not increase or increases slowly even if the accelerator pedal is fully depressed. (The transmission does not shift out of OD) Line B ③ The engine brake suddenly comes in while driving in the D range. (Engine speed increases, similar to Ds range characteristics) Lines B and C to D ④ The engine brake is too strong in the D range. (engine speed is high, similar to Ds range) D or E area ⑤ In the Ds range, the engine brake does not come in. (Engine speed does not increase which is normal in D range) F area or along B and C lines ⑥ The shift line is high in relation to pedal depressed position. The G area is entered when accelerator pedal is fully depressed. ⑦ The shift line is low in relation to pedal depressed position. The H line is not approached even when the pedal is fully depressed. Applicable to D and Ds range operation. Automatic transmission shift characteristic diagnosis. Shift characteristic figure showing engine speed (rpm) vs vehicle speed (km/h) with labeled areas and lines: A, B, C, D, Ds, F, G, H. Y-axis 0-6000 rpm, X-axis 0-150 km/h. Hatched regions indicate shift characteristic areas.

Note: Replace the control valve assembly if the valve or body is scratched. Check the compensation plunger operation No Yes O.K. N.G. N.G. O.K. N.G. O.K. N.G. Automatic transmission (CVT) — shift characteristics trouble diagnosis for low ratio and overdrive ratio faults.
